lines.st: A hunter is walking on seacoast way♪
With homeland left a mile behind, his path lies ahead♪
From his forebears and children, he was torn♪
Into the seas his lover was swept away, floating in the deep, dead♪
A hunter is walking on seacoast way♪
With homeland left a mile behind, they must lament and pray♪
There is no end to his path♪
The road ahead is shrouded in gray♪
A hunter returns by seacoast way♪
His journey ended, his home close at hand♪
Sound of waves past above the spray♪
Why do his feet disobey his command♪
Why ever does his heart still sway♪

opts: Long time no see, Skadi.
Hello, Skadi.

lines.x: Been a while, Doctor.
You were supposed to meet that chatty Liberi, but you don't seem surprised to see me.

opts: You and Elysium are both Rhodes operators.
This is a lot closer to your homeland, after all.

lines.x: Hmm. True.
You're an hour later than Kal'tsit estimated.
I guess the Inquisition's review process is strict, even with an official notice from a Saint.
After only three months, nearly all the town's residents have moved inland, aside from a few workers. Replaced by lots of people from the Penal Battalion.

opts: They must be preparing for something.
They look pretty tense.

lines.x: Ægir contacted Iberia a week ago. This forgotten town has become a lot less quiet since.
Being surrounded by sea scum already sets the young soldiers on edge. Hearing Ægir's voice only made it worse.

opts: It demands a response, maybe caution.
When the undersea nation finally speaks, you have to listen.

lines.x: Turn left at the sculpture, and the sea is a quick ten minute walk.
We'll head straight to the Eye of Iberia.
Say, Doctor, do you hear the sound of the waves?
Skadi turns her head slightly into the sea breeze. Her hair dances in the wind, the same color as the shadows in her eyes.
Their layering has gotten complex. I hear... the sound of stars drifting across the sky. The never-ending song of nature. And a language, forever incomprehensible...

lines.st: Ishar-mla.
I'm here.
The hardship we suffer is ever present.
The life we thirst for is ever present.

opts: Are you alright, Skadi?
Did reconnecting with home put you on edge?

lines.x: It's like I get more nervous the closer I am... Never mind. It's hard to explain.
Maybe I'm sleep-deprived. Ever since our last voyage, I feel like I've inherited Shark's nightmares.
Speaking of the sea, why did Dr. Kal'tsit call you over so urgently?
I don't remember you having anything to do with the ocean.

opts: I wouldn't recall even if I did.
But it doesn't feel unfamiliar, at least.

lines.x: Not much of an answer.
Right, I forgot about your condition. No wonder you gave such an answer.
Oh well. I'm sure she had her reasons.
Let's go. It'll be dark soon.
I would take a step back if I were you. I need to shake myself dry.
Hey there, Dr. Kal'tsit. Not many people can track us Abyssal Hunters. Did Swordfish tell you we'd be coming ashore here in advance?
Elysium deployed a data collector in these waters. It's had a direct connection to the Eye of Iberia's central control system ever since its calibration.
And it can already track underwater targets? That Liberi's not half-bad.
Am I to understand then that Rhodes Island and the Inquisition have deepened their ties?
One week ago, the undersea city contacted Iberia via a special comms frequency, informing them that Ægir would be establishing a 'Waterway' between the Ægir home territories and dry land.
In the face of such a grand project, Iberia could only gather intelligence through the great lighthouse's positioning system.
Evidently, your position as Chief Designer of Warfare did not afford you any knowledge of this.
Tell Kal'tsit what we discovered, Shark.
The captain and I have swum out to where the Stultifera sank three times now. Our last dive was only ten days ago.
The first time, we could still see the exploded wreckage on the sea floor. Under what little light filtered through from the surface, the gilded interior was more like a smudge of paint.
But this time around, the entire thing had completely disappeared, replaced by a cluster of coral, thrumming with life.
I may be accustomed to dreaming, but that felt unreal even to me.
That monument to a lost civilization and its history stood for but a brief time.
The Stultifera Navis has been completely claimed by the sea.
Or to be precise, it's become part of a Seaborn nest.
Their environmental assimilation is accelerating.
At a frightening pace.
But there was something even more noteworthy. We found traces of battle. Recent ones.
A completely barren patch of sea, lacking even marine snow. A warped and shattered stretch of sea floor, clearly pockmarked by micro-singularities.
Only the fleets of Ægir's Legions could be behind that.
As a landwalker, Kal'tsit, the knowledge of and interest you hold towards Ægir are nothing less than extraordinary.
Though separated by the boundless seas, Ægir has always been a part of Terra.
Regardless, Gladiia and I have decided to head to that city.
A risky decision, no doubt.
Perhaps a tad.
Considerably.
As such, we Hunters have been observing it for a while.
Before our final operation, I attended a military conference between the Academy of Science and the Academy of Technology, as the Abyssal Hunters' Chief Designer of Warfare.
Among the battle plans they drew up together, I do not recall anyone calling for a military stronghold to approach dry land with, nor any project involving 'Waterways.'
Ægir has, without a doubt, undergone great changes.
Our contact with the home territories was completely severed after we slew Ishar'mla. It is difficult to determine the truth of the matter from the information we possess.
Yet still hardly reason enough for you to return to Ægir. I don't believe I'm the first to try to dissuade you, either. Didn't your colleague already do so?
Do you mean... Ulpianus?
He would understand that the land holds no answers for us. We must return to the sea.
If his strategy is to maintain a distance from Ægir, then all the more reason for us to return in an official capacity.
...Ever since I awoke from my dream, the waves would tell me, time and again, that the lost should return home.
The Abyssal Hunters must return.
Very well, I understand.
Then I'll go with you, Gladiia.
That would indeed be a rare opportunity for you.
I will be representing more than myself on this visit. More than Rhodes Island, even.
Iberia as well? Even with all that you already bear, they would give you another burden?
It was also my decision.
Rhodes Island's cooperation with the Iberian Inquisition has only just begun. Much like our own.
Kal'tsit stares at Gladiia. The Hunter's face remains devoid of expression, even the blowing sea wind failed to crease it.
After a short moment of silence, Gladiia nods her head.
How many days has it been since we were contacted by Ægir, Irene?
Eight, Su Santidad.
And outside of the necessary deployments, meetings, and to rest, you've spent those eight days here, watching the sea.
They haven't given us a concrete time frame for their next contact. I understand you don't wish to miss their next move, but...
Look at the sea before you, Irene.
The colors of the deep sea bubble up, nearly indistinguishable from one another. Against the vastness of the ocean surface, the sky seems gloomy.
The water crash against the reef, whipping up waves several meters tall, their roars dashing against this small corner of the land.
By Iberian standards, an ocean without a hint of Sea Terror flesh is a gentle one, no matter how noisy it may be.
An old friend of mine was once the most talented bard of the port.
But after that grand port sank into Silence, he fled north, past the starving masses to the Iberian interior, to the small town that would one day become the capital.
I saw him kneeling by the broken town gates, weeping uncontrollably. From his weeping, the town obtained its new name: Perdonidora. 'Forgiveness has left us.'
However, what people needed at the time was not mourning, but consolation. Many prefer to use the shortened form, Perdoni. 'Forgiveness.'
And now, the last Eye of Iberia, the final legacy of the Golden Age, shines bright once more. What do you think that signals, Irene?
Hope and determination.
'Hope and determination.' Of course.
Ah, youth. The determination in your eyes is brighter than Dario's when he was your age. Good, good.
But those words, as inspiring as they are, are often not the fully correct answer.
After half a century of being pushed back, our front is finally advancing. Soon, this will become the new center of the Inquisition.
But at the same time, we must necessarily bare everything to the ocean. To Seaborn and Ægir alike.
Iberia is stretched thin. This country can no longer hide its exhaustion and sickness. It is a simple fact, plain for all to see.
And there is another fact to consider. We can retreat no further.
Maestro—Ah, it's been a while, Doña Irene.
Indeed it has.
Those clothes suit you.

Th-Thank you.
Ah, right, there's been an emergency. The lighthouse just picked up signals of unknown origin.
They seem to be coming from not the sea, but the sky.
Jordi. I think we can already seen them...
Carmen and Irene are fixated on the distant sky. The young clerk follows suit.
He freezes on the spot.
They see a school of fins soaring through the sky.
What are they?
Bionic drones. The Islanders brought similar creations to land, all those years ago.
The matrix of drones crosses the sea in the blink of an eye, matching the height of the towering lighthouse. One separates itself from the formation and slowly hovers before the three.
Its wings open and close like fins, like a speck of light dancing in the void, and soon after an amiable and charismatic female voice resounds from it.
(Iberian) People of Iberia. Do not be alarmed.
Ægir greets you.
I am Clementia, a technology consul of our Institute of Societal and Administrative Supervision.
Carmen holds his lantern aloft, looking serenely at the drone before him.
Two completely different forms of light face each other, as though in a dialogue between two different civilizations—or a confrontation.
Saint Carmen I Iberia, of the Iberian Inquisition.
I believe this is the first official communication between our nations.
Indeed.
You speak our tongue as well as any Iberian.
I installed an Iberian language module before leaving the home territories. I hope you'll forgive me if I ever use the wrong expression.
Moreover, forgive me for having to contact you in this form, Sir... Iberia.
We have no objections to your appearance, Madame Consul. I only regret that it has taken us so long to begin this conversation.
Despite your so-called 'Waterway' project being more than half-complete and close at hand, we Iberians find ourselves in complete ignorance of it.
To make amends, we'd like to invite your envoys to visit our city. Ægir respects the efforts Iberia has made when you were caught up in the Seaborn crisis.
'Caught up in.' Do you mean to say that everything Iberia has faced was merely the aftermath of your crisis?
I do.
Let's not be tied down by formalities, Sir.
We recognize the power of language. Your name itself, Carmen I Iberia, is a symbol. A symbol of courage and valor.
This is why we came to you. We hope that by reaching an accord with Iberia and the other terrestrial nations, we will be able to create a Waterway that links directly to land.
We will clear the stretch of sea between Ægir's home territories and the land, so as to facilitate our forces coming ashore.
...I hope I need not remind you what sending an army to another nation's border implies on land.
Given the enormity of the oncoming threat, we will exhaust every means at our disposal to resist it.
I am more than willing to come explain to you in person if necessary, but it would be easier for you to understand the situation if you were to join us in the depths.
The fleets of our Legions VIII and X are patrolling and suppressing the Seaborn in these waters. They will soon escort you to our city of Milliarium.
This drone formation will continue to notify you of any nearby combat or environmental changes until you have safely arrived at the city.
There may be an unprecedented disaster brewing beneath the waves.
Sir Inquisitor, I worry that though the earth is vast, it is but an isolated island.
The lights of the drone matrix cut out, putting a stop to their sudden communication.
The sea breeze continues to blow as the old man slowly lowers his lantern.
'A Waterway that links directly to land.' She's eloquent, but...
Arrogant. No doubt.
A haughty adult wielding a weapon, loathe to use finesse when dealing with an unarmed child.
Irene, inform our cooperation partners of this most recent communication.
Kal'tsit has already agreed to serve as Iberia's envoy. The Abyssal Hunters should also have made their decision by now, considering they've always had reservations about the Waterway program.
Right away!
I'll also keep the other Inquisitors in Gran Faro abreast of the situation.
I've made a recording of your entire conversation with Ægir, Maestro. Please, take a look.
A-As for whether you approve of me going...
I recall you submitted your application a week ago.
Yes. Since the Ægir fleet is on their way now, I was wondering if...
Never hesitate when you've already made up your mind. It only makes you less persuasive.
When I was younger, Uncle Thiago was always worrying about me. He'd tell me: 'Jordi, you should leave Gran Faro. Leave Iberia.'
But he never told me where to go.
Ægir was always my homeland under the sea, but I had no way of approaching it. I'd never heard the voice of Ægir, or seen its cities.
But now, it's so, so close.
The Inquisition has approved your application, Jordi Fontanarossa. You will serve as an envoy of Iberia, and accompany Irene and Kal'tsit to the Ægir city.
Thank you, Maestro.
Don't thank me. This isn't a reward. Your work since joining the Inquisition has not been particularly good.
I chose Irene because she has already readied herself, even going as far as to renounce her long-standing faith.
And I chose you because you are still bewildered and frustrated. Your weakness will consume you.
Joining the Inquisition was a test. Returning to your homeland is another.
I... I understand.
(Agitated whinny)
This is the deepest part of the trench.
They hollowed out its sides to make their nest. That's why we never noticed it before, despite circling this place several times.
(Wary whinny)
Yes. I smell it too.
This scent... After destroying the ocean's native ecosystem, the Seaborn have created a new one that serves only them.
A comprehensive Seaborn ecosystem, where once it only existed in an oceanic abyss.
Return, Rocinante. I will carry on alone.
(Swaying its neck in annoyance)
The entire continental shelf has changed. I must know who churned these waves.
Tell the Knight to continue the hunt.
We will not be parted for long.
Ulpianus swims into the nest.
He chooses not to use stealth. Luminescent vegetative Seaborn light up every corner of the nest. There is nowhere to hide in the first place.
Atop sets of spiraling blades, minute and curiously-shaped organisms sleep, or perhaps drift along.

The rocks of the seafloor cannot be seen. The fine tendrils of the nethersea brand slowly sway in the current, catching fragments of organic matter. They weave together into thick mats, drowning out any sound Ulpianius makes.
He can sense the changes in his own body. His consciousness extends and his body relaxes, as though every cell in his body is trying to respond to something.
He is clearly an invader, but the entire lair blooms in response to him.
Ul... Ul-pian... Guh... Ul-pianus. Kin.
Ulpianus is nearly able to see through the approaching Seaborn's translucent body. He waits for it to spit out its clumsy words about preying or being preyed on by him, but nothing comes.
The Seaborn thinks about his thoughts.
Finally. You have traveled far.
Here. Sufficient nutrients. Shed your cumbersome flesh.
Return. Grow. Migrate.
Hmph.
For you, I was born, Ul-pianus. Only for you.
I untangle your. Doubt. I guide you. Return to the embrace of we many.
Why did you migrate? Where will you go?
You still waver, Ul-pianus.
You must know we many, kin. You must know.
The genesezoic will soon awaken. The genesezoic must return. A new Cycle. Develop the ocean. Leave the ocean.
The genesezoic will awaken? Leave the ocean?
We many. See. A new path ahead. We hurry—
...What connection is there between you and the affairs of the city?
Based on geological conditions, it's a promising volcano, but the location is less than optimal.
It would be difficult to establish a lengthy energy transportation pipeline while fending off Seaborn attacks.
Did you read the Academy of Technology's report?
Hydrogen from seawater can be used to maintain operation of the Legion fleet, but it would be difficult to guarantee uninterrupted energy supply to the beacon.
It alone accounts for half of the entire city's energy consumption.
It used to be much worse. Our ancestors had to design a bio-energy system for it, maintained through the joint effort of a dozen cities.
In this day and age, a system like that would be attacked by the Seaborn before it could even be activated.
Regardless, we need to find a way to ensure power supply for the beacon.
Maybe Appius would know a way. Let me get in... oh!
Did you forget? He's still in the home territories and our cities are under radio silence.
We can't exactly lay fiber optic cables like we used to, either, what with all the Seaborn out there.
I miss the days when we could get anyone in the country on a holographic call whenever we wanted.
Wait.
What if we modified the output mode of the energy converter?
We have extremely limited experience in this area. There's always a safety risk.
With you in charge of the cognitive link, I believe the risk is reasonable.
Well, I'm glad you're that confident in me, but I'm not. Who knows what an extended conversation via cognitive link with our transformed countrymen would do to both our minds?
There's been plenty of research into this. We'll be good as long as you keep a steady hand.
Even in the home territories, there are very few cases of transformed citizens being granted a final interview. Most are simply euthanized.
Consul Clementia's ideas are radical, as always.
Which is exactly why I followed her here.
By sorting through the threads of reason via conversation, we can grant them a chance to leave a final trace of their sapience in the world.
And what better way to send them off than with a stimulating debate?
Sadly, most of the survivors the Sea Patrol brings back are already transformed beyond recognition. Precious few have the opportunity to return to the city to receive a final interview.
Say—
Hm?
We're witnessing history unfold here, aren't we?
Or maybe we're just doing our jobs. I trust that the Institute of Development Planning's faith in you is not misplaced. Make your preparations.
Thanks. See you in the isolation chamber.
Excuse me—
W-What the? A Catastrophe? Under the sea?
Taken by surprise, the young man loses balance and falls, but an even stranger sight stifles his cry of surprise—
He sees the passersby float into the air, swimming through it like fins through water, paying little heed to the swaying city below.
As things return to normal, some land and walk away—continuing their conversations like nothing had happened—while others continue to swim forward through the air.
He recalls the Ægir rituals the people of Gran Faro had told him about when he was little.
Get up, Jordi.
R-right!
Doña Irene?
Next time you go out, remember to put on the device that the Sea Patrol gave us when we landed yesterday.
Right, I remember that from the briefing videos... Which one was it again...?
The fourth one. The technicians at the port reminded me.
They make regular adjustments to the gravity settings, as part of what it takes to maintain the low-gravity environment of Milliarium.
They said it's like a fin adjusting its swim bladder.
So you went to tour the port.
Yes. I was interested in their ships and docks, their weapons... those massive ships that sail like fowlbeasts through reefs and cliffs.
I saw traces of the Stultifera Navis in them. Breogan really did recreate Ægir technology in Iberia. Perhaps...
Perhaps we should have sailed our proud armada out into the vast oceans, rather than parading our gold-gilded ships along the coasts as a deterrent to those dukes and pashas.
Breogan...
You look nervous, Jordi. So nervous that you missed the point entirely.
I'm just feeling a little out of place...
This place is stranger than I imagined. The air is thick and heavy, the ocean above my head sends a chill up my spine, and the glowing plants along the streets make me dizzy.
Plus what happened just now...
Physical discomfort, I could deal with, but there are so many oddities here I can't even begin to understand yet.
The briefing videos Ægir provided were very detailed, but there's still so much that I couldn't comprehend, even after rewatching them so many times.
Food moves on its own without a fork. Clothes clean themselves without having to be removed. These things make sense, kind of.
But Edible Sagas? Crunchy Theorems? I just can't wrap my head around those. The best I can do for now is print out one of those emergency nutrition supply devices.
All my life I've known nothing but Gran Faro. Is this what it's like to...?
No, it's not quite like an Iberian from a backwater village seeing the capital for the first time. Not that the City of Gold still exists.
The difference here isn't one between a small town and a metropolis, but... almost between two entirely different civilizations.
I tried to ask someone for directions, but couldn't understand what they were saying, or whether I was being rude to interrupt.
I don't even know how they see me—an Ægir in name only.
I have my concerns, too.
You're worried too?
You of all people know what Iberia did to the Ægir after the Profound Silence.
I don't know what kind of country Ægir is, how its people view that period of bloodshed, or how they view me—an emissary with blood on her hands.
But you didn't—
I'm here representing the Iberian Inquisition. I've seen the files on the slaughters... Each report on its own may be thin, but in aggregate...
We committed many injustices while cleansing the influence of the Church of the Deep, and the Islanders were the most affected.
I still believe the Inquisition was in no position to be lenient, not when the country itself teetered on the brink.
It was our collective decision that we should go to any lengths to ensure the nation's survival in a time of extraordinary crisis.
But that does not lessen our crime.
I don't believe you need worry about that.
Growing a massive vesicle to resist a blast from Class III weaponry, and covering the retreat of the other Seaborn into the depths of the trench...
How is it even possible for biological tissue to be made to resist the forces of a micro-singularity?
The Sea Patrol brought back tissue samples. They've already been sent to your lab through the transport tubes.
At least they haven't yet developed a collective resistance to Class III weaponry like they have for Classes I and II.
The performance of Class III weaponry is demonstrably superior.
But even more concerning is that an individual Seaborn has evolved measures specifically to counter it. They're more... organized than before.
You've been analyzing the Seaborn's adaptation to various weaponry ever since Milliarium set sail. Did you already foresee this?
Advisor Blandus?
To an extent. But we need more time to confirm our theories.
Thank you, Secunda.
Your reports are always so detailed I dare not blink for fear of missing something. Little Handy, get me some eyedrops.
Ignore that command, Little Handy. Just a bad joke.
If there's nothing else, I'll be going. I need to speak to the technicians about the damage suffered by our ships.
I'd rather you stay just a while longer, unless you're hurrying to get some rest after work.
Isn't it somewhat ironic, considering you've also received numerous health warnings but haven't seen a doctor yet?
...Very well. Still, I'd appreciate it if you could hear me out. It won't take long. I just need to confirm something—
Have you read Gladiia's report?
I have.
Then you should know about Ulpianus. I thought you'd at least show some sort of reaction.
Of course I'm concerned about the reappearance of the Abyssal Hunters after five years of silence. Especially when the Waterway Program is at a key juncture.
And Ulpianus's unusual activities are of particular note.
He clearly made contact with Gladiia's group and is aware of Milliarium, but has made no attempt to reestablish communications with Ægir.
Meanwhile, we've heard from our Iberian sources that he's also declined to work with Iberia.
He may be proud—arrogant even—but he is no fool. He must have his reasons for acting alone.
Regardless, I intend to find out where he is and what he's planning.
That utilitarianism, that impatience... he's rubbed off on you. We had hoped you'd be better than that.
I can only pray your decision-making will be more prudent while tracking him.
Nevertheless, I have faith that you'll keep me updated on his whereabouts.
What are you talking about, Advisor Blandus?
Neither of us is part of the Abyssal Hunter program anymore.
You're the weapons technology advisor for the Waterway Program now. Aren't utilitarian, impatient decisions the usual business in your field?
And shouldn't you be getting information about Ulpianus from Milliarium's official sources? Why involve me?
Of course, of course.
Just a momentary lapse in judgment. I can't fault you for carrying out your duty as Praefecta of the Sea Patrol.
I just ask that you remember what Ulpianus did for us, for Ægir.

Yes, speaking.
What? Missing?!
I'm on my way.
Who's missing?
One of the technicians on the Waterway Program has gone AWOL.
And you are...?
Avitus, from the Institute of Prehistoric Studies, Academy of Science. We study the documents and artifacts of the prehistoric civilization.
Irene, emissary of the Iberian Inquisition.
Jordi. Jordi Fontanarossa.
A pleasure to make your acquaintance.
And don't worry, I didn't come to lecture you. I simply felt it was my duty as a citizen of Ægir to free our landbound friends from their needless concerns.
Needless concerns?
What I mean to say is, Ægir understands that Iberia took measures proportionate to the extraordinary nature of the times.
We can find similar periods in our own history, or even that of the glorious prehistoric civilization.
The exclusion of outsiders as a necessary means of maintaining social order appears to be an inevitable step in the progress of civilization.
Besides, we welcome our displaced countrymen to return to the sea, just as we respect the decision of those who have opted to leave.
The consequences of their choices belong only to them.
I appreciate Ægir's magnanimity, Avitus, but the 'consequences' they suffered were...
No, it's nothing. Thank you for clarifying.
You're welcome—
Mr. Avitus, our landbound guests, please be informed that this area has been designated for investigation.
You are advised to proceed to another area unless you have unresolved matters here.
A Hand of the Saint?
The Penal Battalion used them to clean up Seaborn remains, but I never knew they could talk.
Apparently, our people who brought them to land did not have time to demonstrate their full capabilities.
Multi-purpose Support Equipment, commonly known as a 'Little Handy.' They currently offer 972 different function modules, excluding obsolete ones.
One can sometimes tell their affiliation just by looking at the parts and paintjob. This one, for example, belongs to the Sea Patrol.
Er, should I reply?
Um, we're very sorry, Little Handy, er, sir.
That would be unnecessary. It may be equipped with a voice, but that doesn't mean it's intelligent.
I assumed that with your technology, maybe...
It's true that the topic of simulated personalities has fascinated many a researcher.
The first attempt was created 230 years ago and continues to operate to this day. In fact, I can put in an application for you, if you wish to talk to it.
However, whether from the perspective of neuroscience, psychology, sociology, or anthropology, simulated personalities have proven to be rather disappointing in terms of their benefits to research.
Perhaps I've gone off topic, though. In short, they've remained the stuff of niche research, and we do not consider them suitable for widespread application.
The goal of all technological development is to empower humanity. What purpose is there in turning a machine into a human?
I see. Fascinating.
You said this Little Handy belonged to the Sea Patrol?
Only they would fit such powerful loudspeakers onto one, when quieter means of communication are available. I suppose you could consider it retro?
What sort of organization are they? Do they frequently undertake such investigations?
The Sea Patrol is part of our Legions. They perform a supporting role in combat, as well as regular reconnaissance, patrol, security, and disaster response duties.
Emergency sweeps like this are rare, but with the Waterway Program at such a critical moment, it's only reasonable for them to be on high alert.
Little Handy, am I allowed to remain here?
It is not recommended, but not forbidden.
Then I'll wait here. I'd like a chance to speak to the Sea Patrol.
Very well. And you, Jordi?
I'll be going. Do you mind if I ask you some questions?
Of course not. There's a limit to what I can answer outside of my specialty, though.
Perhaps I could show you to a place better suited for such a conversation.
The Sophontheatrum.
Did you sleep well, Doctor? You seem a little haggard.

opts: It's the air pressure. And humidity.

lines.x: Yes, the Ægir seem to have retained some of their aquatic traits.
Are you not feeling well?

opts: Just a little short of breath.

lines.x: Is it serious?

opts: Don't worry, it's nothing.
Kinda, yeah.

lines.x: Let me know if you feel any discomfort.
Besides...

opts: 'Besides'?

lines.x: To be frank, I thought there may be another reason for your reaction.
Does anything about Ægir or the depths of the ocean seem familiar to you?

opts: A little, but—no, not here.
Maybe, but only vaguely.

lines.x: So you do remember something?
I'm accustomed to your usual silence, but now it's making me a little uneasy.
I know it might be difficult, but remembering even the slightest detail could be of great help to us all.

opts: Sorry, I can't remember anything else.

lines.x: It's okay, just try to relax, Doctor.
Seeing the Preserver has inevitably hastened the pace at which you recall the truth... Even though I had hoped you would not touch upon what you'd forgotten so quickly.
No one knows when your path of suffering will end. Not even yourself. In that, we are all powerless.
What we do know is that you've had an encounter with the abyss. The ocean is yet another path to your past, and we are finally upon it.

opts: You seem excited.
Do I detect... satisfaction?

lines.x: Yes. I've wanted to set foot in Ægir countless times.
Until now, I have waited on the shore, and drifted on the waves.
I have witnessed lighthouses and the armada turn from ideas to reality. Breogan had never given up on reestablishing communication with Ægir through such means.
But all his efforts ended in failure. Ægir never welcomed home their prodigal son, much less opened their doors to outsiders.
But now, they are even more eager to cooperate than I had anticipated. We need no longer waste efforts to persuade a proud and aloof giant.
I can't deny feeling happy about that.

opts: You don't look particularly happy.
Is there something else troubling you?

lines.x: I'm afraid there is.
Ægir's proactive stance indicates that they have a plan for making contact with the land.
But even the most moderate of their measures may seem too radical for landbound civilization. A single gesture of goodwill could cause a massive storm.

opts: We should intervene as soon as possible.
The Abyssal Hunters are a good entry point.

lines.x: The Hunters' situation warrants concern, too.
To Ægir, they are neither emissaries nor guests.
Having participated in the most radical project in Ægir's history, and 'come back to life' after being declared dead for five years... I worry that their return home would only cause—
Trouble?
Controversy?

opts: Skadi.
Specter.
You're back.

lines.x: Where's Gladiia?
A somewhat troublesome fellow called her away.
I thought your priority upon returning would be to get checked up. What could possibly be more urgent?
No idea.
But once Gladiia's done with her interview, I guess Skadi and I are gonna go do some stupid check-up.
Swordfish and I are fine. Shark's changes might be a little problematic.

opts: There's no Originium in the ocean, is there?
Does Ægir understand Oripathy?

lines.x: I read some medical papers before I underwent the surgery.
Even back then, they had advanced knowledge of medulla regeneration and replacement. They should be able to help her.
You needn't worry, Doctor.
I've inspected myself in the mirror on that great ship. On returning, those endless dreams still creep up on me like bubbles, but they've also become as light as bubbles.
Besides, I feel like I'd have more regrets about losing those dreams.
But for now, I'm as lucid as I've ever been. And luckily for you, I'm even in the mood to take you on a tour of the city.
Interested?

opts: Yeah!
Let's go!

lines.x: Did you think we were touring a museum? You were looking at the instruments like they were exhibits.

opts: The third place I mistook for a museum, then.
Why is gravity reversed in there?
What's with the water flowing around the pillars?
What's that space that warped my fingers?

lines.x: There are some things even I can't explain.
Technology has advanced a lot in five years.

opts: Wait, that tower...
There's a launchpad at the heart of the city?

lines.x: Good eye, Doctor, that's a beacon launcher. One with plenty of history.
You know about it, Shark?
My parents took me around the home territories when I was little. There are similar structures in many cities.

opts: I thought Ægir children were raised collectively.

lines.x: I was, but Shark has a special relationship with her biological parents.
They're more like friends than parents.
They reached a consensus with the Institute of Incubation to bring me along whenever they worked on a new dome project.
I spent most my time in the Institute of Incubation, but I was able to see the cities, learn, and even work alongside them.

opts: So, what's this beacon launcher for?

lines.x: Exactly what it sounds like: it launches beacons to mark the Waterway.

opts: Those are some huge beacons.
How big is this Waterway anyway?

lines.x: Ægir had almost mastered the oceans around 300 years ago, efficiently using natural energy in its various forms and recording every seafloor trench in our navigational charts.
Then we cast our sights on another ocean, one brimming with stars.
Our fleets and even our cities were meant to cross that boundary that should never have existed, and light the fires of civilization beyond the barrier.

opts: Beyond the barrier...

lines.x: The Preserver was right, Doctor. Kristen's achievement is not beyond Ægir's reach.
The sea and the sky are closer than we thought.
People back then tried plenty of things: surveying and deciphering the electron clouds, developing new technologies and materials, exploring forms of life that can adapt to space...
This beacon launcher is a product of that era.
It was intended to capture even the faintest signals from the stars, and plant beacons that would never fade from our star charts.
Nanomachine swarms would form networks between the beacons, allowing us to traverse vast distances in an instant... or so we dreamed.
You never told me any of this before.
Lucid moments were hard to come by on land.
But yesterday, I dreamt once again of walking from the Sophontheatrum straight into that epoch.
Then, I saw Laurentina dance.
She leaped from the waves to the stars, stirring up star dust that trailed the hems of her dress. From afar, the voice of the Master of All Creation answered her in song.
The universe and imagination know no bounds, and Laurentina's dances will never cease.
Specter closes her eyes, as though sinking back into the dream.
Mm...
What is it?
Just trying to picture it.

opts: It's a beautiful dream, Laurentina.
Perhaps that day will really come, Laurentina.

lines.x: Before pushing open the door to the stars, they wanted to prepare themselves for any threats beyond the barrier... That is, until the Seaborn appeared and interrupted the dream.
We poured all our efforts into studying them, setting aside all else: art, science, and previous visions for the future...
The Seaborn fundamentally changed Ægir.

opts: What did you do before becoming a Hunter, Skadi?
What did you do before becoming a Hunter, Specter?

lines.x: Me?
I was the best technician at the Institute of Facility Management. I could keep an entire city's underwater lighting array running on my own.
The Institute of Development Planning's career proposal was pretty much what I was thinking, so I accepted.
Working by yourself can be lonely, but it's not such a bad thing playing with the giant fins in the reefs, or lighting up the ocean while daydreaming.
I was just an apprentice sculptor.
The Institute of Development Planning provides tailored career proposals for each citizen, which are usually in line with our wishes.
I suppose I would have gone into dome design, sculpting, or dance.
Say, Kal'tsit and Doctor, we've shared a lot of info during our time together, but we've never talked about our old lives.

opts: Because they've changed.

lines.x: I guess you're right. Sorry for bringing up something so heavy on a tour.
We have all the waves in the world hanging above our heads. Would you care to share a dance before dealing with all this bother, Doctor?
Gladiia.
Gladiia's hand instinctively goes to her weapon as the woman approaches with a ceremonial sword in hand.
But all she does is put her arms around Gladiia's shoulders. Gladiia smells stimulants in the air.
Ægir do not make a habit of greeting each other with physical contact. She could not remember even her mother ever doing so.
But this woman's action felt completely natural.
Consul Clementia?
I was not aware that this was the standard salute between Chief Designers of Warfare.
Nothing makes me happier than seeing the Hunters return as lucid as ever.
Your soldiers' reactions when they first saw me suggested otherwise.
The operation to eliminate the Firstborn cost Ægir all of its Abyssal Hunters.
Secunda made many rescue attempts, but was unable to penetrate the rampaging mass of Seaborn that guard the region after the death of the Firstborn.
Your mother, Horatia, had no choice but to publicly declare you dead.
But I know her. She doesn't really believe that.
Or perhaps Consul Horatia, foremost of the Institute of Scientific Development Planning, simply could not accept that I had died for nothing.
I know her, too.
But I don't think it is appropriate for a meeting between two Chief Designers of Warfare to revolve around a complicated mother-daughter relationship.
Of course, of course.
Look at the city.
The Consul's Pensivarium sits high in the city. The seawater hangs just above their heads.
The transparent dome serves as a gentle mirror, warding off the deep and foreboding void while granting the Ægir the solace of introspection.
Beneath the dome, the entire city is bathed in a blue hue. The beacon launchpad stands tall at its center, while ships glide in and out of the distant docks like schools of fins.
The sea itself has no color, only darkness. Milliarium stands in its murky depths like the vigilant eye of civilization, solitary and luminous.
We once had many such cities. One by one, the stars in the sea went out, becoming sustenance for the Seaborn.
All that's left on the seafloor are their colorful, eerie nests.
I won't deny that the Abyssal Hunter project was a failed gambit. The death of the Firstborn did not stop their evolution.
So I want to know what solution Ægir has come up with next. What you have come up with.
What is this Waterway Program?
Gladiia, I didn't mean...
I'm simply part of an even bigger gambit, one with even higher stakes.
The weapon classification scheme remains nothing more than a strict control over weapon usage to delay the evolution of the Seaborn.
This time, however, we have taken into account every weapon at our disposal or under development.
A mix of conventional ballistics and Class I weapons initially proved highly effective. The Seaborn were unable to comprehend why they were being burned by the seawater around them.
But it took them only a year to adapt. 11 months in, we accelerated the adoption of Class II weapons, which operated on completely different principles.
Et cetera, et cetera.
Until their evolution outpaced the ability of the Academy of Technology to innovate.
Yes. At day's end, all we're doing is merely buying time. Ægir has committed vast resources to study the Seaborn. To eradicate them at their origin, we must first comprehend them at their source.
The information you've brought back is highly valuable, and Breogan's discovery on land corroborates the Academy of Science's research.
The Seaborn and the Feranmuts share the same genesis.
Is Ægir coming ashore to seek memories even more ancient?
Not just that. The Seaborn share a common genesis with the Feranmuts, but much about them is very different. Unnatural, one might say.
Their recent activity demonstrates a clear sense of purpose. They have quickly abandoned their reliance on the sea and begun to migrate towards land en masse.
Left unchecked, it is not out of the question that all of Terra could become infested with Seaborn.
We already saw hints of that in Iberia.
Thus, for Ægir, the land is both a source of answers and a solemn responsibility.
Horatia has issued new war orders on behalf of the Institute of Development Planning, and Milliarium has come bearing new Class IV weapons from the Academy of Technology.
They are a form of biological weapon, very different from all previous ones. Designed to induce death in nearby Seaborn nests and newborns, while driving away adults from the area.
We have successfully located most of the nests in the target region. The ones near Iberia are the only ones yet unmarked.
Once the beacons have been placed and the weapons activated, we will finally have cleared an entire region of Seaborn, and established a route back to Ægir's home territories.
Consul Horatia would not have permitted the possibility of failure. Yet all I see is a city under siege, teetering on the brink.
Yes, she did tell me herself that 'the Waterway Program will not fail.'
I do not know how many other possibilities she considered, and I hope I never do. For me, this city has always been the entirety of the Waterway Program.
But that is no longer true. The changes in the past month necessitate a review of our plans.
The Seaborn have rapidly adapted to our new weapons, even evolving measures to protect their kindred from Class III.
But a successful extermination operation should have left no opportunity for them to communicate with other individuals. They seem to have obtained their inspiration elsewhere.
Almost every mutation of the Seaborn against humanity has been abetted by the twisted efforts of corrupted Ægir.
Yet, the Seaborn lack true intelligence. We could toss all our prototype weapons into their nest, and they wouldn't dismantle or study them.
The Church of the Deep may be able to train them to attack an army or a city, but bringing about rapid biological changes is another matter entirely.
Unless they're not being influenced by the Church of the Deep, but by another Firstborn.
I see you've read my belated report.
Indeed. Ulpianus claims that the one we eliminated was not the only one.
The Abyssal Hunters were the first and last Ægir to enter the Firstborn's lair.
Even now, the Seaborn defend the ruins with a frenzied fury. We have not found a way to get through.
Amongst the Hunters, Ulpianus spent the longest time there, enough for him to witness the 'truth.'
You're still deliberating on whether to believe him.
Yes. It could be the key to solving the mystery of the Seaborn. Or the path that leads us to destruction.
Could a Hunter survive for months on Seaborn remains and remain lucid? Would a lucid hunter willingly stay with them, rather than returning to civilization?
There are too many unanswered questions about Ulpianus and that fateful operation.
And so, I regret to inform you that Ægir has no choice but to welcome its Hunters home with a tribunal.
I thought we had designated this area for investigation.
Madam Irene? From Iberia?
May I ask to whom I am speaking?
Secunda, Praefecta of the Sea Patrol. I regret not being able to greet you personally upon your arrival yesterday.
The Sea Patrol is currently investigating the disappearance of a citizen. We have not requested assistance from Iberia, and respectfully request that you exit this area as soon as possible.
I would like to assist as myself, not as an envoy.
Your offer is appreciated, but unnecessary.
I want to—
Any news from the other teams?
One moment, getting the video feed now.
Ma'am—
No unusual movements at the port.
Tsk.
Alert the other teams to monitor the closed civilian gates and keep a close watch on the circulation system around town.
Roger.
You're okay with talking about this in front of me?
This is part of our regular duties. There's no need for confidentiality.
But you had your Little Handies clear the place out.
Only to prevent unnecessary harm to civilians.
If you insist on staying, you'll be responsible for your own safety.
I'll—
The missing civilian is Tullia, a scientist from the Waterway Program, responsible for recording and calibrating data on Seaborn nests.
She went AWOL 30 hours ago and hasn't been seen since.
Could it be... the Church of the Deep?
That's what we intend to find out.
Her disappearance has not affected the overall progress of the project. As important as recording and calibrating the nest data is, her role was by no means irreplaceable.
If the Church's goal was to assassinate someone to disrupt our plans, they would not have picked an ordinary data scientist.
Unless—
She learned something she wasn't supposed to.
I need to inspect her home.
You're sharp, Madam Irene, but I would recommend against coming with us.
I'm simply investigating our common enemy in my own way.
...Suit yourself.
No one has been in here the whole time?
Affirmative.
No signs that the Little Handy's logs have been altered, either.
Excuse me, how do I view those records?
Grant her the permissions.
Yes, ma'am.
Ah, very nice!
Secunda takes a device from her belt and dances her pen across its surface, leaving trails like a labyrinth of seaweeds.
What's your Praefecta doing?
She scribbles whenever she's thinking. Says it helps her organize her thoughts. It's a habit she's had ever since she was a researcher.
No one else can decipher what she's writing... or drawing? Not even that's clear. She's the only one who can parse it.
(Books are arranged by thickness, shell decorations by size, and data downloaded from the Sophontheatrum by time.)
(Seaweed cream to prevent dry skin. Is it because Milliarium is approaching the coast?)
(There's enough to last three years.)
(An ordinary Ægir woman. Cautious, timid even. Matches the data from the Institute of Development Planning.)
(Those hydroponic tanks—)
What are these boxes for? Why put them on the wall if they're empty?
Yes, the hydroponic boxes may provide a lead.
Scan all equipment in the room related to plant cultivation.
Yes, ma'am.
Also, pull whatever you can from Tullia's files. Education, employment, hobbies; anything that might be relevant.
She previously worked at the Institute of Ecological Art, with a focus on the cultivation of endangered seaweed.
To quote straight from the IEA's files...
'The artist has carefully cultivated these deep-sea seaweeds to weave a dynamic, ever-evolving portrait of life as they grow.'
These hydroponic boxes were Tullia's belongings. Images of her artwork are also available.
How beautiful.
They look like they've been left empty for quite some time.
According to the files, she joined the Waterway Program after a reassignment.
She wouldn't have abandoned her old life for no reason.
True. Her disappearance may very well be related to her career change.
How's that scan coming?
Nothing unusual with the cultivation equipment, but we found an operating log in the integrated terminal.
Two days before she went missing, she downloaded some data from the beacon tower control center and made a backup.
We were unable to locate the file itself, though.
Try to backtrack her steps. Contact the control center and trace the data from their download logs.
If she was still going to work before she disappeared, couldn't her friends or colleagues know something?
Perhaps.
What's the situation on her social relationships? What about the person who made the report?
We've arranged a questioning.
Anything I can help you with, Jordi?
Sorry. I have so many questions, I don't know where to start...
This, er, metal tree? Do I just ask it? I've heard legends of ancient trees acting as repositories of knowledge, but I've never seen anything like this...
This coral-shaped integrated terminal is the culmination of centuries of Ægir's knowledge and culture.
Everything we think, know, create, appreciate, and even the questions we have yet to answer... they're all here. It's the heart of the Sophontheatrum.
And what is the Sophontheatrum, exactly?
Conceived and established by Mártus, the great scholar of anthropology and prehistoric civilization, it is a public facility that has had a profound impact on the nation.
Aside from the mutually-supporting atoll city clusters above sea level and the modular city networks in the trenches, most of our other cities have unique forms and designs.
Yet even so, nearly all of them have chosen to adopt Mártus's design and establish their own Sophontheatrum.
Look around you. This is a place for people to think, doubt, and debate, eventually coming to an understanding and making a decision. A coliseum of wits, if you will.
The most complex equations, the most ancient drawings, the most beautiful songs, and the most well-traveled roads were all born here. I believe this is exactly what Mártus himself wanted.
You keep mentioning this Mártus... what kind of man was he?
Simply listing all his contributions to Ægir would take an entire day, perhaps more. But I do happen to have some insight into his personality.
Oh, you knew him?
No, no, he lived more than two centuries ago. He'd been gone from Ægir long before I was born.
But our professors liked to speak of his eccentricities. He was unmatched in theoretical and deductive reasoning, yet was always reluctant to draw a conclusion.
Even on issues where the answer was plain to all, he would refuse to put the question to rest until after an extended debate with himself.
Some would say that trapping himself in endless debate showed he cared too much about the value and meaning of things. But is that not in our human nature?
Which is why he built the Sophontheatrum. We needed a place where we could engage in intellectual discourse, both with ourselves and with others.
I wouldn't say I'm up for a debate, but I do have a lot of questions. Should I ask you, myself, or this terminal?
It seems our little discussion must give way to a greater showdown.
What do you mean?
They're holding a tribunal, and it concerns three of those you arrived with.
The Abyssal Hunters?!
Oh, hey, is that our landbound friend I spy? Aw, he looks worried about us.
(Waves)
He won't see you from that far away.
Others are looking this way, though.
This tribunal has drawn more attention than expected.
We Hunters always have, and now they even have the lovely story of our return from the dead.
Like it or not, the Abyssal Hunters are in the spotlight now.
This tribunal will be our final 'checkup,' yes?
Frankly, they're getting tiresome.

opts: Are you okay, Specter?
Take it easy, Skadi.
Consider it our warm welcome.

lines.x: The Hunters need to keep a low profile. We must also act with prudence, Doctor.

opts: We always find ourselves in delicate situations.

lines.x: Indeed. This time, we're not merely mediating between two countries, but between land and the sea. Two vastly different civilizations.
Following the tribunal, I will formally address Ægir's consul to foster cooperation between land and sea. But for now, you'll need to assist Gladiia independently, Doctor.
The situation remains uncertain for both the city and ourselves. Certain matters become more difficult if I am placed at the center of attention.

opts: But no one will be watching your 'assistant'?
So I should play it by ear?

lines.x: Yes, but be careful. I would prefer not to have to change our objective in the middle of negotiations with the Consul.
Dr. Kal'tsit sure uses a lot of big words, doesn't she?
Does she ever get straight to the point?
Our dear Dr. Kal'tsit thinks too much about too many things, and not nearly enough about how to convey that she cares.
Wouldn't you agree that more direct, Ægir-style communication is better, Doctor?

opts: Absolutely.
Kal'tsit has her reasons.

lines.x: Don't worry, Shark and I will protect you.
Say, where's Captain Gladiia?
I imagine she'll show up with Consul Clementia. After all, she's the one who will be taking the stand today.
Excuse me! A-Are you Laurentina?
I had a hunch I'd find you here, advisor.
The environmental sample test results are out. We need you to take a look and see if we need another round of genetic testing.
Oh... Sorry, didn't see your message.
You seem preoccupied by the Abyssal Hunter's tribunal.
You used to work at the Abyssal Hunter Lab, right?
I'd rather not talk about it.
But yes, I was researching gene splicing methods that would provide a safer and more effective way to perform the Hunter modifications.
But you didn't like the program?
It's not about liking or disliking it. It's that it failed to produce results, despite all the sacrifices we made.
But what matters now is that they're alive, Gladiia and the others. I have to be here, if only for them.
Were you two close?
Hah. If only she thought so, too.
Magister Avitus. May I sit here?
Of course, Cassia. Be careful, though; recovery from embryo separation surgery takes time.
This is Jordi, a guest from Iberia.
Thank you. And hello.
Oh, er, hello.
There are more participants than I expected.
Even when the Institute of Facility Management held a tribunal to discuss modifications to the city's unit framework, only two departments were involved.
The tribunal system exists to solve problems, after all. The only reason to involve more departments is when the problem is too complex for one.
But the Sophontheatrum is already half-full. Is the return of the Abyssal Hunters really such a big deal?
It's a project that took two centuries of debate before implementation. Its philosophy shook the very foundations of Ægir, and its every progress tucks the string of Ægir's future.
Besides, the Waterway Program is reaching its most critical juncture. Even the smallest thing out of the ordinary is worthy of attention.
Simply reading a report is not nearly enough to come to an informed decision. Consul Clementia needs the counsel of experts in multiple fields.
I'm sure that many have come of their own accord, though. The return of three Hunters is bound to cause unease at such a sensitive time.
I'm not really here for the tribunal, but I heard you might make an appearance, so...
And who might you be?
Oh, sorry.
I'm Lucilla. I had the honor of meeting your parents once.
Rulers and ink... Are you an architect working on the dome system here?
I-I transferred out of that last year, but I'm still with the Institute of Facility Management. Now I maintain the city unit framework and measure ocean currents.
'Envy not the sun, for we have given ourselves the eternal stars and the infinite sea.'
When I was little, the foster parents at the Institute of Incubation took us to hold a performance here.
We created a matrix of tiny optical machines, representing neural activity with changes to the brightness of the dots.
We'd rehearsed thoroughly, but the Sophontheatrum malfunctioned on the day of the performance, and the whole place went dark. Except for those little machines.
But a couple in the audience worked their magic, and ribbons of light fell from the dome, as if arms reaching out to invite our little machines to dance.
It was so beautiful that the blackout almost felt intentional...
Ha, I can picture the smug look on their faces. In fact, I wouldn't be surprised if they really had something to do with it.
When the Institute of Development Planning did its career assessment, I applied for the dome system without a second thought. It was only then that I learned they were famous dome designers.
...That was the only time I saw them.
So they were at the right time and place to inspire you.
But you said you transferred out of dome work. Why, when you've already formed such a special bond with our big, transparent friend above?
Lucilla! The tribunal is about to start. You want to sit here?
Sorry, my colleague is calling out to me. Anyway, welcome home, Laurentina, and I wish you the best of luck.
Pretty much everyone's here...
Hey, where you going, Doctor?

opts: The Hunters draw too much attention.
I'll just be watching from the stands.

lines.x: Kal'tsit's words really got to you, haven't they?
As you're all well aware, Milliarium has successfully made contact with Iberia. As such, the Waterway Program now enters its most critical phase.
A thread of light leaves the massive coral-shaped terminal to surround her, forming a giant ethereal ring in the air that dances to the consul's words.
More threads weave together to form cities and tides, Hunters and Seaborn, transmuting words to images and giving substance to sound.
The crowd quiets down.
And at this crucial juncture, we also welcome home our surviving Abyssal Hunters: Gladiia, Skadi, and Laurentina.

The results of their physical examinations and Gladiia's mission report have been uploaded to the Sophontheatrum's terminal and are available for your perusal. I'm sure many of you have already done so.
Concerning the irregular circumstances therein...
I now call upon Consul Gladiia—leader of Ægir's Honor Exercitus, and Chief Designer of Warfare of the Abyssal Hunters—to answer your questions.
You navigate through several rows of Ægir and sit in one of the back rows. With plenty of room around you, you have a clear view of everything happening in the Sophontheatrum.
The tribunal has already begun. The speakers do not raise their voices, yet every word is clear in the massive ring-like structure. Gladiia steps forward, drawing everyone's gaze.
Suddenly, you hear footsteps so soft they are almost inaudible, like a shadow from the somber dome touching the floor.
Not a sound.
Or I'll crush your throat.
Consul Gladiia, is it in your opinion that the issue does not lie with the battle itself?
Correct. What you see before you is a projection of the battle plan. If anyone here notices a flaw I haven't, I'd expect it to be pointed out.
No, I have no objections to the battle plan. It was the most suitable one, given what we knew at the time.
However, I would like your opinion on what we knew at the time.
I have to admit that Ægir's knowledge of the Seaborn back then was deeply flawed. As such...
What are they trying to say?
Still not used to their way of thinking, are you? You never did like thinking much, even back home.
They mean no ill will. They're simply seeking the truth they need.
Identify every flaw and question all perfections. The most heated debates will uncover truth in its full beauty and reveal the right way forward.
Just leave it to Swordfish.
If you don't want to listen, just take a nap.
...In conclusion, our greatest error when designing the warfare five years ago was failing to make allowance for flaws in our knowledge.
Until every last truth of Terra is revealed, we must admit that our understanding of the Seaborn will remain flawed. Our warfare design must reflect this revelation.
I concur. Your conclusion should be incorporated into our future battle plans.
It has been an honor to reflect upon this critical battle and reach a fruitful conclusion with you.
That said, the battle five years ago is not our only concern.
We have more pressing issues to worry about.
Your physical examination has revealed mutations that cannot be ignored. The same goes for Skadi and Laurentina.
I do not doubt your loyalty, but we need to know how assimilation by Seaborn genes will affect your behavior.
Apologies for my candor.
Milliarium will soon identify the locations of the remaining nests. Then, the Seaborn will be driven out by Class IV weapons, opening up a Waterway.
Is there any possibility that the Abyssal Hunters will become an enemy of Ægir, and of yourselves?
Hunters out of control pose a greater risk to Ægir than the Seaborn.
The Abyssal Hunters are the flipside of Ægir. Created in Ægir's most despicable attempt at salvation, they are now both feared and pitied by their creators.

opts: Abyssal Hunter Ulpianus, I presume?
Shouldn't you be down there too?

lines.x: You're much calmer than I expected. And you know who I am.
It seems Gladiia has built a relationship on land with more than just the one doctor.
I've never seen you in any of the operations. You're not an Inquisitor, nor an Islander.
The doctor from Rhodes Island brought you to Ægir, but kept your identity a secret.

opts: I'm just Kal'tsit's colleague.
I'm also from Rhodes Island.

lines.x: You may introduce yourself however you like.
However, the point is that you can help the Hunters.

opts: Why don't you just go to Skadi?
Why don't you just go to Specter?
Why don't you just go to Gladiia?

lines.x: This is not her forte.
You mean Laurentina?
This is not her forte.
Now is not the time.

opts: You don't trust the Hunters?

lines.x: On the contrary. I do trust them. But I also know them all too well.
Skadi acts before thinking. Laurentina has not been lucid for long. Gladiia will inevitably be the one to make decisions.
I've already shared much of what I know with her. She should be on guard. She knows the potential restrictions, but nevertheless decided to return to Ægir.
Overeager to return to a familiar environment, she is oblivious to the fact that she has placed herself in the spotlight, where all that can and cannot be trusted have the power to scrutinize her.
Now is not the best time for the Abyssal Hunters to come home. Their decision is a big gamble.

opts: Why did you sneak into the city, then?
Sounds like you care about them, after all.

lines.x: Gladiia and the others are not in a good situation.
They underwent modifications, but unlike me, they were not part of the Abyssal Hunter project's core research. Explaining the changes in their bodies will be difficult for them.
I'll pass on some information to you, and I'll need you to help Gladiia if necessary. Convey my warning to her later.
As for the rest—
May I say I few things?
...Blandus. He's here too?
Blandus?
Isn't this just like the old days? You and Ulpianus used to get into arguments with the research staff while I was napping.
Your comments would be much appreciated, Advisor.
You flatter me, Consul.
I was the technical advisor for the Abyssal Hunter project, responsible for the gene splicing research used in the modification process.
In other words, at this exact moment and in this exact place, I am the leading authority on Abyssal Hunters.

Gladiia, Laurentina, and Skadi have been fighting on dry land too frequently and for too long. It is indeed cause for concern regarding their physical condition.
As such, your concerns are not without reason. But my answer is this:
No—
They will not become Seaborn.
The hall goes silent.
Blandus clears his throat.
Let me try to explain this in layman's terms.
Under ordinary circumstances, Seaborn genes exhibit abnormal dominance and will irreversibly overwrite any preexisting traits in their host.
In Abyssal Hunters, however, we have managed to extend the timeline of this process until it far exceeds the natural lifespan of any given Hunter.
Furthermore, the Academy of Technology made a critical breakthrough, allowing us to implant an 'auto-adaptive junction' in all the Hunters before their deployment to eradicate the Firstborn.
The auto-adaptive junction was implanted at the splice point between human and Seaborn DNA to assist the Hunters in more closely monitoring their Seaborn genes.
If the Seaborn genes exhibited abnormal dominance, the junction would signal the body to resist their attempt at taking over.
While there has undoubtedly been some degree of decay of their auto-adaptive junctions after five years without maintenance, the changes they've undergone remain well within acceptable ranges.
So you say, Advisor, but there is a record of a failed auto-adaptive junction surgery in the Abyssal Hunter Lab archives.
...That was Ulpianus.
The archives should also note that multiple clinical trials have demonstrated that the incident did not produce any adverse effects.
For this, I gladly take full responsibility and will answer any queries.
Now that the Abyssal Hunters have returned to Ægir, their condition will be under control.
Am I to infer that you already have an effective means to contain their mutation?
There is an Abyssal Hunter Lab in the city we can reactivate. I will personally perform maintenance on their auto-adaptive junctions.
What of Ulpianus?
How can we be sure that the Hunter who has refused to return to Ægir will not become Seaborn?
Or that what we fear hasn't already happened?

opts: Sounds like you've just become the target.
Sounds like you're pretty dangerous.

lines.x: Hmph.
It's only natural they would be skeptical.
It is both human nature and a hard-learned lesson to be wary of what you cannot control.

opts: They're not the only ones suspicious of you.
I'm equally curious about your motivations.
Did you really come out of concern for the Hunters?

lines.x: Before the sinking of the Stultifera, I shared my discovery with Gladiia: there is more than one entity like Ishar'mla amongst the Seaborn, those known as Firstborn.
The Firstborn are stirring. It, or rather They, are about to awaken.
I don't know how many or how soon... but it's close. Too close.
This is why the Seaborn have begun migrating towards land. I've ventured into many nests recently, and as a whole, they are feeling a sense of crisis.
Perhaps not even the ocean contains enough nourishment for the awakening of a new god. Will it become another Profound Silence for the land, or another calamity for the seas?
The great migration almost perfectly coincides with Ægir's Waterway Program. One can only speculate about Milliarium's role in all this.
Is that not reason enough for me to visit this city?
The Seaborn have proliferated independently, driven by their instincts. But they came from the Firstborn, and cannot defy their fundamental nature.
In other words, even if the death of the Firstborn failed to halt their evolution, it should not have caused the rapid emergence of new, previously undocumented traits.
I believe I already addressed that in my report.
Yes, the Multiple Firstborn hypothesis. A logical enough explanation, but that is exactly why I am skeptical of Ulpianus.
Our well-laid plans ended in failure. The three surviving Hunters washed ashore on unfamiliar land, before being reunited with the Hunter who was last to leave the nest.
The information Ulpianus brought—that there are more than one Firstborn in the ocean—is more than shocking, it's highly 'effective.'
Effective in explaining why the Seaborn's actions have become more disruptive despite your success with the mission objective. Effective in addressing all our questions.
And why does that make you skeptical?
Because it's an answer so perfect that it's either the incontrovertible truth, or was designed specifically to put our questions to rest.
Whether or not to believe the information he gave you will have an enormous impact on our warfare design.
If Ulpianus lives, then the most effective way to verify his claims would be to track him down, perhaps even question him directly.
You have been quiet, Advisor Blandus.
Do you have anything to add regarding Ulpianus?
You were once students under the same mentor, and it was your trust in him that led you to join the Abyssal Hunter project despite your reservations.
But I also trust your knowledge and ethics as an Ægir. You need not recuse yourself.
All I can say, Madame Consul, is that I can be accountable for every word I say here at this tribunal.
I will not intentionally put forward a hypothesis in his favor, nor will I make any conjectures that could hurt him.
That is all.

opts: Sounds like you two are pretty close.
I guess even a loner has friends.

lines.x: Ulpianus does not answer. The questioning continues, but Blandus says no more. Suddenly, he looks up towards you, as though he's sensed something.
You meet his weary gaze across the length of the Sophontheatrum.
He seems to have noticed something. The consul next to him and several other Ægir catch that peculiar moment, and some of them follow his gaze.

opts: Em...

lines.x: You look to your side and find the seat empty.

opts: Don't mind me. Just a landlubber here to watch.
Sorry, am I interrupting?
Do I need to speak?

lines.x: The tribunal has arrived at an adequate conclusion.
Gladiia, Skadi, Laurentina, and the rest of the Abyssal Hunters have made tremendous sacrifices and contributions to Ægir.
It is a blessing for Ægir that new ideas continue to emerge from their knowledge and experience even after five years.
They have demonstrated that they are both innocent and safe, and that their return at this stage of the Waterway Program is not a threat, but a reassurance.
Yet now our attention turns to another of the surviving Hunters, Ulpianus.
He has concealed his movements for reasons unknown, and has attempted to influence the other Hunters. Both actions clearly contravene his duty.
Based on our present information, we are unable to determine his motives, goals, or even whether he still qualifies as Ægir.
Ulpianus must be found. If his intentions are indeed benign, as Gladiia claims, our suspicions must be cleared through proper questioning.
Those whose questions have been answered may now leave. Those whose questions have not may continue their inquiries.
Do you have anything to add, Consul Gladiia?
The coral-shaped terminal at the center of the Sophontheatrum slowly closes and sinks into the ground. The space is now flat and empty, like a stage ready for a new play.
Gladiia stands alone on it.
I have told you all I know.
I have no objections to the tribunal's conclusion, nor to the suspicions cast upon Ulpianus.
People begin to file out, many turning to nod towards Gladiia as they reach the exit.
The remaining audience continue to ask questions, and Gladiia responds to each one, whether she is able to answer or not.
The water caustics cast by the dome weave a picture of light and shadow with the ring of light surrounding the space, dancing on Gladiia's expressionless face.
Even as an Ægir, I find this hard to understand sometimes.
They fling words at her like spears one moment, then thank her for her contributions the next.
They're sincere, you know. Science requires people to separate facts from feelings, and they're good at it.
I know. I'm just... *sigh* Never mind.
As for Captain Ulpianus, all they have are suspicions. Swordfish, the Doctor, Kal'tsit... someone will think of a way.
What next?
We wait for Swordfish. Which, unfortunately, will take a while. *sigh*...
You're sighing too?
She used to visit the Sophontheatrum to watch plays whenever there wasn't a mission. Sometimes I went with her.
Captain Gladiia...
She's fairly well-versed in the arts. Her mother's influence, supposedly.
Unlike everyone else, however, she liked the highest, furthest seats, so I would always end up having to sit apart from her.
Why?
For her, keeping the distance helps filter out the noise. Ignoring some of the finer details makes it easier for her to fully immerse herself in the story.
Right now, though, she's the one at center stage.
She’s lost her accustomed sense of distance, becoming the subject of judgment, scrutiny, and discussion. No one in her position can remain unperturbed.
I'm starting to get why I sighed.
Why?
Because only now does it really feel like we're back.
*pant* *pant* Gotta hurry!
Caulerpa archaeosphaeridium? Here? Who else in Milliarium is cultivating endangered seaweed?
Hypnea fenestralis, Halymenia fimbriatus, Palmaria purpureus...
Wait, the color of this Sargassum pennata... I cultivated it myself...
Why are you on the streets... it's too dry. You'll die.
What happened here? Did someone do this?
Where are you migrating? Is there something—
W-Wait!
They're gone?
This pipe...
Wah!
This is...
*cough* Argh—
(What's going on?)
(The rumbling... Is the city moving?)
(Something's moving—who's there?)
(Vacuole inflates merrily)
I hope I'm not interrupting anything.
Not at all. Lucilla and I were just performing regular inspections and maintenance on the city's unit framework.
Still no sign of Tullia?
C'mon, Lucilla, it hasn't been that long since we made the report.
I can tell you care about her. Did she mention getting into any trouble recently?
She isn't one to look for trouble. I can't imagine her offending anyone.
What you say certainly fits her profile. Did she ever talk to you about the Waterway Program or beacon data?
No.
I wouldn't say we're close, exactly. We talk from time to time, but she's more of a loner who prefers to work on her own stuff.
Wait, is she a suspect?
Sorry, maybe I'm making her sound suspicious...
B-But it's probably just stress. There's been fighting almost every day in the six months that Milliarium has been away from the home territories. Everyone here is on edge.
The Church of the Deep continues its attempts to sabotage the Waterway Program. We've eliminated many cultists over those months.
And until they were exposed, they appeared just like any of our countrymen.
But she'd have no reason to—
Well, to be honest, I've never quite understood why she chose to join the Waterway Program.
What do you mean, ma'am?
You already know that she used to work at the Institute of Ecological Art, right?
The IEA moved to another city when this one evacuated its original position and received new battle orders.
Right, and she had the chance to go with them to continue her work. But instead, she chose to—
Sorry to interrupt, but could you explain more about this evacuation you mentioned?
You remember, don't you Lucilla? You were working on the dome at the time, so you had the best view of the whole thing.
...Yeah.
It was one of Ægir's largest volcanic power stations, feeding countless laboratories, and Milliarium was responsible for its construction and defense.
The city was parked beneath the power station. Through the dome, I could see its red glow illuminate the ocean for miles.
The heat it generated attracted many different creatures. It was the largest endangered plants reserve in Ægir.
And the ideal environment for Milliarium's ecological art to flourish.
Then, that light turned dark...
Seaborn?
Yeah.
A year ago, swarms of Seaborn arrived on the ocean currents, and Milliarium ordered an emergency evacuation.
Without the natural environment to support it, the Institute of Ecological Art, like other related institutions, was forced to relocate.
But there was a full Legion in the city. We could have retaken the power station.
War orders for the Waterway Program had just arrived. Milliarium was in fact being strategically redeployed.
I came here with the Legion after that.
The lives of so many of our people were changed... including Lucilla.
By the way, I transferred from the dome to ground work because I was afraid of heights... Sorry, off-topic.

So you mean to say that Tullia might have had ulterior motives for joining the Waterway Program?
That is a serious accusation.
I don't believe it either.
No, no, I'm just telling you what I know.
But... it's undeniable that there are a hundred thousand souls in this city, and not all of them are fully committed to the Waterway Program.
Most just carry on with their lives, not understanding the kind of path we're clearing out in the ocean.
Even our Iberian guest here came to learn more about the Program, didn't she?
Whether Tullia is linked with the Church of the Deep or not, the fact remains that she made an unauthorized copy of data on the Waterway Program.
We'll have to find her to learn why.
But Milliarium isn't that big. She'd have been found by now, even if she were hiding between dome layers or in the corners of the circulation system.
True. She may well have already left the city, but we were unable to find her bio-signature in the nearby waters.
Nor were there any unscheduled ship movements at the port. And she couldn't have gone beyond our sensor range in just a few days using civilian deep-water equipment.
However... the city did make a short movement around 30 hours ago to receive the Iberian mission. Perhaps...
Could she have left the city at that time?
...So, the Columbian scientist Kristen Wright broke through the barrier for the first time in more than ten thousand years.
The hole she pierced hasn't completely closed, even after a year. Ægir's surveillance of the universe through this opening continues, and so far no anomalies have been detected.
I tried to stop her, to no avail.
We still have time, but it's running short.
Ripping apart the electron clouds with a high-energy beam... We were not aware of any energy sources on land that could support such a brute-force measure.
Her method came from the same place as Ægir's technology: prehistoric civilization.
She had discovered sarcophagi beneath Trimounts that preserved that civilization's spark, and managed to obtain the trust of its custodian.
Before leaving the home territories, I had not expected to find anyone with your profound understanding of both Ægir and prehistory from the lands.
You have demonstrated, once again, that there is still much about Terra we do not understand.
Fins trapped in the depths lose their perception of light. Likewise, our nation must continue to explore, even if we can no longer support grand expansions.
The stargate, the twin moons, the barrier, the ruins in the mantle... There is much on this planet that was not formed naturally.
Our voyager fleets, having traversed to the far side of the planet, have even detected highly unusual terrain concealed behind the distant dark fog.
There, the sea currents abruptly cut off, as though they had reached the end of the ocean.
That is merely a hotbed that has long since cooled. The most important prehistoric relics lie in the Ægir heartlands. You know this, Madame Consul.
The prehistoric ruins beneath the seabed are a gift from the ocean to Ægir. They nurtured your civilization, and have granted you power and knowledge far beyond the terrestrial nations.
And with them, responsibility.
As you say, Dr. Kal'tsit, responsibility.
