When did the land dwellers first start calling themselves 'human'? When they lit the first fire with Originium? Built their first city with stone and mud?
On land, self-awareness and identity were established naturally over time. Even today, humanity is often defined in the narrowest terms.
For the Ægir, it was conferred. We were not the same race as the Predecessors, but we inherited their identity as we inherited their mantle.
Thus, Ægir was born with a responsibility to humanity. The Seaborn will not compel us to abandon this duty; on the contrary, they add to its weight.
You are indeed the right consul for Milliarium, Madame Consul.
Where is this coming from...?
Your temperament. As Ægir, you are patient and kind.
But that does not diminish the arrogance in your thinking; that is to say, Ægir's arrogance.
I do not believe our conversations about the hole in the barrier, the ruins in the mantle, and even the future of civilization itself, are merely small talk to lighten the mood.
Just as I do not believe Ægir will rely on passive solutions. After all, isn't Millarium's departure from its home territories a proactive effort to create a new line of defense?
So, my question is, Madame Consul: what is Ægir's endgame for the Waterway?
...I have already told Gladiia Ægir's answer, that which you would call arrogant. You have the right to know as well.
There is much on this planet that was not formed naturally... and Ægir does not believe they are unrelated.
Be they wonders like the stargate and the twin moons, or calamities like Originium and the Seaborn, these phenomena cannot be random products of spontaneity. They must be interconnected in some way.
To solve the Seaborn problem at its source and stop Terra's crisis, we need to fully understand Terra, including the hidden secrets of the land.
But those are for the future. If you simply seek my answer for what's at hand...
My endgame for the Waterway is to ensure that hundreds of thousands of Millarium's inhabitants and tens of thousands of Penal Battalion troops in Gran Faro live to see Ægir's main force arrive.
Distress signal.
From the squadron tasked with beacon deployment at Nest 37.
You're sharp.

opts: Saved me the effort of looking for you.
Do you always come and go without warning?

lines.x: How much has Milliarium told you of the Waterway Program?

opts: Thought you'd care more about the Hunters.
Thought you'd care more about your circumstances.

lines.x: The tribunal has demonstrated their innocence, and they are not alone.
Besides, Gladiia’s return to Ægir and its conspiracies are of her own accord.
Neither of us is being followed or under surveillance, so my movements have not been exposed.
Let's continue where we left off at the Sophontheatrum.
The war between Ægir and the Seaborn has gone on for more than two centuries, but both sides have set their sights on dry land at almost simultaneously.
The sudden migration of an entire species, and a change in direction for an entire nation. Perhaps, deep down, they stem from the same cause.

opts: Sounds like you don't trust any of Ægir's plans.
You still have your reservations about us, Ulpianus.

lines.x: I know more than I've told Gladiia. This extra knowledge forces me to be cautious about Ægir's past and future actions.
But it's too soon for her to know, and not your place to know at all.
Our only concern for now is the Waterway Program.
A path connecting the land and the sea... Neither that doctor nor the Inquisition fully trust in this, or else you would not be here.
Clearing the seas with powerful weapons to buy time for the main force from the home territories to arrive. Ægir's warfare design is always simple and effective.
But there have been anomalies in the city that we cannot ignore.

opts: The Church of the Deep?
I heard about a missing person.

lines.x: That consul is a formidable woman.
During Milliarium's voyage, she has culled many of the corrupted and thwarted their sabotages, while the two escorting Legions have sustained far fewer losses than expected.
Everything has gone well... Almost too well.
Now, a staff member in the Program has gone missing at the most crucial stage. This is just the beginning.
I'm looking into it, and will eliminate any potential threats.
I can enter most places and evade the Sea Patrol's regular rounds with ease.
But I fear the problem runs deeper.
I would rather not barge into an important lab or the consul's Pensivarium, unless I have to.

opts: I'm not your messenger to the Hunters, Ulpianus.
You should work with Rhodes Island, Ulpianus.
After all, we can help you move around the city.
After all, Ægir sees you as a threat, don't they?

lines.x: Rhodes Island wants to work with me... and watch me, I assume? So be it.
One more thing.
...Skadi.
If you truly care about the Hunters, keep a close eye on her.
One hundred fifty-six, one hundred fifty-seven...
I've never seen a port this busy.
Are things not going well on the frontlines?
(Humming♪)
You're still in the mood for music?
Why not?
Soon we might be too busy for it.
There you are, Captain Gladiia.
You went to see Consul Clementia with Dr. Kal'tsit right after the tribunal, right? How did it go?
Well enough, but I was only there to make introductions. They had much to discuss, and I had no reason to stay.
I did have a brief discussion with Clementia on the current plans for the Abyssal Hunters, though.
Ægir's Waterway will open soon. Until then, the Hunters will fight alongside the two Legions accompanying Milliarium.
See? I was right.
Uh-huh.
I'd rather be out there killing monsters than sit through another debate.
So what's the mission and when do we move out?
The command structure and battle plans are pending discussion with the legion commanders.
Skadi—
Hey, that's the emergency alarm, right?
What happened? A Seaborn invasion?
I am Consul Gladiia. Status report.
Yes, ma'am.
Two squadrons from Legion X were sent to deploy the beacon at Nest 37 today, as planned.
But we just received a distress signal. They were attacked by Seaborn on the way.
The Seaborn were prepared?
Unclear. The situation is still developing. The Academy of Technology hasn't had time to produce a report.
Are there any corvettes on standby in the port?
Always. You can claim one by logging into the terminal.
Thank you. Keep me posted about the squadron's coordinates. Once close enough, the Abyssal Hunters will disembark and swim into battle.
Roger.
Laurentina, with me.
...Skadi, you stay behind.
Captain?
Don't overthink it.
The contact between land and sea is paramount. Kal'tsit can't be in two places at once, so I need you to keep the Doctor safe.
This is an order.
Received.
That's...
Laurentina's group is embarking for battle? But they just came back.
It has something to do with the alarm. The fleet deploying the beacon at Nest 37 was attacked by Seaborn, and they're going out to help.
You know the Abyssal Hunters?
I do.
We had a brief partnership on land, facing challenges and battling enemies together, including the Seaborn.
That's... impressive, given their temperament and fighting style.
I'd go up and greet Consul Gladiia myself if we weren't in the middle of an emergency. I haven't had the opportunity since they returned.
Does everyone in Ægir know the Hunters?
Of course not. But I once worked with them... and even aspired to join them.
Someone from your team said you used to be a researcher.
My expertise in genetic engineering qualified me for the Abyssal Hunter program. I studied under the head of the project, who was also the first Abyssal Hunter.
At the time, few were compatible, and even fewer successfully underwent the operation. But he and Gladiia had already achieved spectacular results.
Their most remarkable feat was a three-day battle with the Seaborn in a hydrothermal vent that bought time for two energy survey cities to evacuate.
The Seaborn evolve constantly. With the use of weapons strictly regulated, they were the safest and most effective countermeasure.
I watched them leave and return every day.
I watched them lie down in the regenerative gel, exhausted, waiting as I recorded the data, tested their functions, and repaired their bodies. Those were the costs of victory people could not see.
I'm not entirely sure what motivated me at the time, but one day I submitted an application to become a Hunter to the Institute of Development Planning.
...I'd have done the same.
What?
I mean, I'd have wanted to apply myself.
To see your mentor and everyone else risking their lives like that... Nobody would want to feel that they weren't doing enough.
You seem fit enough, and as a researcher, you are beyond familiar with their functions and combat style. Wait... then why didn't you—
Because my application was rejected.
But why?
(Shakes head)
I didn't ask. And my mentor wouldn't have answered.
So you joined the Sea Patrol instead?
Because it's the unit that works most closely with the Hunters?
Yes... and monitors them.
The corvette is ready for you.
Alright.
Sorry, Irene. I shouldn't have bored you with my past.
It's fine.
Back to work. As the fleet battles the Seaborn, we must also locate the missing scientist for answers. The Sea Patrol's objective remains unchanged.
As for you, Irene. You're staying with us.
Wait, what for?
We won't be in the thick of battle, but it's still dangerous. It's the Sea Patrol's duty to keep you safe.
I appreciate it, Praefecta. But I'm good.
I'm not afraid of the Seaborn. Every Inquisitor has Seaborn blood on their hands.
Former Inquisitor, as I recall from your files.
I appreciate your assistance during the investigation. Like I said, you're sharp... and brave.
But you didn't come to Ægir to fight. Go back and stay with the other emissaries.
Are you implying that the war between Ægir and the Seaborn is beyond a land dweller's comprehension?
...Yes.
Your concerns are justified, but trying to comprehend is why I'm here.
There are many things I... as an Iberian, need to understand: Ægir, the Seaborn, true war, and everything that's happening in the depths of the ocean.
The land isn't far from here. Ægir has only a single city in this region, but beyond it lies the entirety of Iberia.
Don't worry, I don't intend to get myself killed at sea.
Such maturity at your age... You must have a good mentor.
From what I've heard, better than yours, at least.
...Prepare a set of deep-water equipment for Irene.
Let's go.

lines.st: Audio Log #13
(Unknown language, unknown time)
The reactor of a United Starline starship still burns so brightly.
Since the atmosphere control center crashed, we've been shrouded in dull gray clouds. A radiant sight like this certainly lifts the spirit.

To my pleasant surprise, Valeria was also on the starship. I discovered once she landed that she had come to bid farewell to her mother. Ever since joining the stargate modification project, she has been completely absorbed in her duties. It was surprising to see her still concerned with worldly matters.
If she still has a mind for such things, perhaps we might finally talk about that wedding that's been put on hold for the past ten years?
…I soon dismissed that thought as foolish. The woman I once knew now stood before me like a stranger. I could no longer discern what she was thinking.
As she stood before her mother's life support pod, her expression was one I had never seen before. It was neither sorrow nor regret... it was envy.
What could she possibly envy about her dying mother? Was it the peaceful release offered by the machine's simulated world?

lines.x: You're still here, Jordi? Everyone else has left...
Sorry, I didn't know there would be a tribunal today.
That's okay.
The search logs... you've asked quite a few questions. 'How big is the ocean?' 'Is it bigger than the land?' 'By how much?'
'Can Ægir cities float to the surface?'
'How do the Ægir tell whether it's day or night?'
Is this how you've been trying to understand Ægir? I fear it may confuse you even more.
Mr. Avitus... I'm not sure why, but I feel really excited.
In the Inquisition's nautical charts, the ocean extends only a few nautical miles from shore before giving way to unknown regions marked in grayish-blue.
In some areas, we couldn't even get near the shore.
But your charts are so detailed. Geological features, energy pockets, local fauna and flora... There are so many pages, I don't even know where to start.
The forests at the heart of the ocean, icebergs that never melt, thermal vents deep in the trenches...
For a moment, I thought that the land, sea, and sky were all mine for the taking, that I need simply reach out. Like everything was made for me.
Sorry, that sounds really arrogant, doesn't it?
Quite the opposite, young man. It is but a most basic desire.
But I...
The young Ægir looks up and gazes at the coral-shaped terminal. Holographic images and words leap up and down, illuminating his soft eyes.
I've been in Milliarium for a whole day now.
But I can't handle your tech, I don't get your discussions, and I'm even lost with directions.
The tribunal that just happened... I have no idea how you guys settled your disagreement.
I did a search for Breogan, the ship designer, who I thought was my ancestor. I saw his face, heard his voice, and looked at his designs.
Is there really anything I have in common with him? Would his descendants really turn out to be someone... like me?
I couldn't find a single sign that says I belong here.
Ægir is like a lighthouse, bright and tall. I always knew it existed, but when I finally followed its light and reached its base...
Why do I feel lost instead?
Breogan. It's been many years since his name was last spoken.
He was a remarkable man, even amongst the Ægir. He was the most talented designer in the Academy of Technology, but left everything behind to go ashore, right when his country needed him most.
Even this city is connected to him, albeit in a somewhat unusual way.
How so?
It was through this city that he went on land. The Institute of Development Planning even tried to penalize a few consuls for failing to stop him.
Er...
Listen, Jordi. I cannot ascertain whether you are truly bound to him by blood, though such a connection holds little significance.
What matters is that there can be no doubt that you are Ægir.
I know I am. But how do I prove I belong here, if I can't even trace my own heritage?
A true Ægir needs no proof of that.
But I understand. We all search for answers in the past.
I'm no different.

I can say with a straight face that I am one of Ægir's foremost authorities on prehistory. I firmly believe in the weight of history, and that it contains truths and patterns that will show us the way forward.
(Mutters) But what if its a way of destined destruction for Ægir?
...I'm sorry, what was that last part?

opts: Are you alright, Skadi?

lines.x: Huh? Did you say something, Doctor?
Sorry. I was just thinking about Shark and Swordfish.

opts: They're probably on a killing spree right now.
They're probably worried about you.
Chin up, Skadi.

lines.x: I'm not worried about them. They're strong.
Nothing good ever happened to those around me, which is why I was always alone—until I joined Rhodes Island.
Now it's happening again.

opts: I'm here.

lines.x: Mm.
Thank you.

opts: Are you afraid you'll bring disaster to the Hunters?

lines.x: Maybe.
But we hunters aren't afraid of the bad end we might bring to each other.
Because we all share the same grim fate, we can trust each other with our lives.
This time though, it might not be just the Hunters.
I could bring disaster to all of Ægir.

opts: You won't. I believe in you.

lines.x: Mm.
That's what Captain Ulpianus always said.
He would explain our battle plans in great detail to everyone else, but with me, he simply said he believed in me.
...He believed I could complete my mission even if I couldn't memorize those complex strategies.

opts: And do you believe him?

lines.x: Mm.
He was always so reliable and had an answer for everything.
I remember the songs he wrote before battles. He said we could use them as codes to communicate because the Seaborn didn't understand music.
But I just liked the songs.
He had a great sense of direction, too. He could always lead us all back, even when we were lost in those maze-like nests.
We could always count on him to point the way, until...

opts: Until the final battle?

lines.x: Yes. The 1st and 4th Companies sacrificed themselves to clear a path, while the 2nd held off the Seaborn that were rushing back.
Captain Ulpianus led the charge and soon arrived at the target, the core organ of the Firstborn.
Then we lost his signal.
I thought he was dead, so it fell to me to deal the killing blow.

opts: But he wasn't.

lines.x: Yeah. I don't know what happened that day.
I still don't know why he didn't come back.
And the more I don't know, the more I feel like I need to do something.

opts: Trust your warrior's instincts, Skadi.
Remember? Woosh, pow, thud?

lines.x: Ha. Rush in with a 'woosh', knock them down with a 'pow', and finish them with a 'thud'.
I remember.
Arrived at target coordinates.
We came as fast as we could. Though not fast enough, it seems.
This is a graveyard.
Giant shipwrecks and Seaborn remains lie entangled in a grotesque embrace. Pale flakes descend like snow.
While some have given it a more romanticized name, marine snow, it does little to hide its cruel nature: fragments of organic matter, both Seaborn and human.
The sweetness of blood and the stench of death, intensified by the cannon's lingering heat, spread with the falling flakes.
The Abyssal Hunters' strong sense of smell magnifies all of this, and Specter cannot help but cover her nose.
This shouldn't be unfamiliar to you, Shark.
The fleet pins down the Seaborn with superior numbers as the Hunters flank and eliminate the swarm's core. We've used this tactic often.
This is the same scene we see each time we leave their nest.
I know... perhaps we've spent too much time on land.
It's not a pleasant thing to recall.
One engineering mothership, ten battleships, numerous smaller ships... The wreckage positions on the seabed indicate they stayed in formation. They were not taken by surprise.
There are signs of gravitational distortion on nearby rocks. They tried to stop the Seaborn using force fields.
But the Seaborn were faster. The ships weren't even able to form a crossfire.
They fought with them in close quarters...
Something's not right.
What's the matter?
The squadron's destination was Nest 37, but all the ships appear to be headed for Milliarium.
Besides, now that the squadron is destroyed, why haven't the Seaborn made their next move?
Swordfish.
Let's go.
Ngh—
Ugh—
Alright, grab my hand.
I did tell you to stay on the corvette.
Deep-water equipment may keep you from being crushed by the water pressure, but even the Ægir are at the mercy of the ocean currents without proper training.
I could use some help, once you're done laughing.
Imagine you're dancing with a rowdy partner. Counter her moves and find your balance. This is how you fight the currents.
You're light. It shouldn't be hard for you.
...Don't look at me like that.
I'll meet you up ahead.
A broken lighting array...
Trace amounts of organic tissue on the broken metal frame... Human, and Seaborn.
Find anything?
Some tissue that matches the sample provided by the Institute of Development Planning. It's Tullia.
Milliarium was parked here around 40 hours ago. Based on the location of the array, she had left from the circulation system's sewage exit at the edge of the city.
...But why did she leave?
It's hard to reconstruct the scene with what's available. The currents have already washed away most of the clues.
I'm afraid we won't find out if Tullia was a cultist with a grudge against the Waterway Program or what happened to her from here.
This is two hours away by ship from Milliarium's present location. Even with deep-water equipment...
She couldn't have swum back.
Based on the remaining tissues, it seems she had encountered a Seaborn.
There's no way she could have survived.
Right.
A dead end.
That's... the direction the Abyssal Hunters went.
The battle may be fiercer than expected.
The Waterway Program is in its final phase. Once Nest 37 has been located, we can clear the Seaborn from this entire area and open up a Waterway.
This can't just be a coincidence...
Could the incident with Tullia have something to do with the attack on the fleet?
Or maybe her incident is just a red fin to distract the Sea Patrol.
There are too many new uncertainties, as if everything's sucked in a giant vortex.
...Let's go back to the city for now.
Hard bank to port, full speed ahead! Go around that ridge and turn back. That should lose a third of the Seaborn on our tail.
Ngh—
Sir, the Seaborn have breached the hull!
They're inside the ship!
Keep the evasive maneuvers going, helmsman! We'll deal with the ones inside.
Camouflage coating down to 10%! We'll be fully visible soon!
Buy as much time as you can!
Yes, sir!
Gugh—
Take care of the rest, Shark.
Abyssal Hunters?
Gladiia.
I recognize you, ma'am.
Have the other ships made it safely back to Milliarium, Madame Consul?
Thank goodness...
The more of our elites we save, the more strength we'll have for what's ahead.
Are you the commander of this vessel?
Yes, ma'am.
We were ambushed. A large swarm of Seaborn left their nests and appeared on the squadron's route... Virtually unheard of in previous beacon deployments.
We couldn't reveal our battle plans. We had to retreat.
Our ship was the fastest in the fleet. The most logical tactic was for us to delay the Seaborn while the main fleet retreated.
How many survivors are there on your ship?
Twenty-six... Less than one in ten.
Why the silence, Madame Consul?
I see.
I'm sorry, commander. Your ship and your twenty-six men are the only survivors.
All Seaborn inside the ship have been eliminated.
There's no better way to tell you, so I'll make it brief... the thousand or so men whose retreat you were covering fell into an even bigger ambush.
We happened to see your ship weaving through the complex terrain as we arrived.
But what you couldn't have seen is the Seaborn had you completely surrounded. You were just chasing your own tail in their trap.
We failed the mission.
Yet we were the ones who lived. The losses are... much worse than expected...
But you did live.
Lift your head, Ægir.
You and your men are just as much Ægir's elites, and you will give your strength to what's ahead.
For now, Laurentina and I will bring you back to the city.

lines.st: Voice Log #21
(Unknown language, unknown time)

Valeria has been back for half a month now, but I fear the package from Talos may never arrive. Those precious prototype technologies and biological specimens were lost, along with the civilian-grade environmental simulation unit I ordered.
No notification, no incident report. The cosmos simply slammed the door in our faces without warning.

Poor Mother. She must endure the life pod a few days longer. This may be selfish, but I was the one to insist on bidding her farewell in a simulated environment. Until the moment she entered hibernation, she wanted to see her grandchildren. How could I tell her the grandchildren she envisioned simply do not exist?
No doubt she still dwells in that glorious age where we journeyed among the stars. It's the only way she could see that continuing life doesn’t just add to the pain.

I chose Talos Union Industries' products for their reliability. Mother's too sharp. If the simulation tech was even a generation older, she'd see right through it. I should have realized the risks of interstellar transport sooner.

Valeria's leave is all used up. As she left, she was still talking about her grand mission, showing not a single sign of regret. I don't know if she was leaving her regrets behind, or never had any in the first place.
One day, I too will sign my name on the consent form, and shut down Mother's life pod.

lines.x: What brings you here?
I came to see you.
You've just undergone embryo separation surgery. The Institute of Facility Management should have allowed you to take time off to rest.
The surgery is as non-invasive as it gets, you know. There's no need to rest that long.
I can't tell whether you care more about me or the little fellow in the incubation chamber, Magister Avitus.
Of course I care about you, but any newborn in Ægir as it is now deserves attention as well.
After he's born, will you choose to raise him personally, or give him to the Institute of Incubation?
I don't think about that sort of thing. It's too far away, too distant.
I went to the hospital before I came here.
The hospital? Why?
You're aware of the battle yesterday, yes? Surely you've seen the news?
Two fleets and over a thousand legionnaires went into battle, but only twenty-six returned.
The Waterway Program is at a turning point, and Milliarium has endured its greatest sacrifice since its departure. There have also been quite a few abnormalities in the city as of late.
What is it you're trying to say?
The Sea Patrol are already looking for you, aren't they, Cassia?
About that missing data scientist.
Magister Avitus.
You know, of all the documents in your collection, the one I like the most is 'The Death of Life.' Despite its lack of scholarly value.
I only ever found that diary by accident.

More than three hundred years ago, four great researchers studying the prehistoric civilization made incredible discoveries in the mantle ruins, bringing back countless precious technologies and data to Ægir.
Compared to all those blueprints and theoretical papers, a transcript of voice-recorded diary is hardly worth anything.
And so it gathered dust in the Academy of Science's databases. Until, thanks to your curiosity, it was deciphered and turned into a popular reading material.
Merely the musings of an ordinary Predecessor on his endless journey.
But I loved the story of that Predecessor.
I enjoyed seeing reality through his eyes.
How the connections between countless stars collapsed, how the trust between myriad races crumbled away. Goods were in short supply, technology was lost, philosophy and art fell silent...
No matter how much hope and vision went into their great undertakings, they ultimately faded into silence... Just like those nanomachines interred within the beacon tower.
Did you empathize with his despair, Cassia?
You've never shown a trace of that emotion, though. You even chose to have a child born into a world that could face ruin at any moment.
That isn't the choice of someone who's lost all hope.
I don't feel a shred of despair. Really.
That old-fashioned, dispirited, middle-aged man... It's hard to imagine how I could empathize with him at all.
I simply appreciated how, by borrowing from his past experiences, you ingeniously tore apart the magnificent facade covering the eyes of today's Ægir.
'We must accept that the things and qualities we were once so proud of have no use now. Perhaps they have always been flawed, and this calamity merely exposed them.'
Once free of worry over survival, people will begin to consider 'value.' We pray that our thoughts, our deeds, even our very existence will have value.
Every one of Ægir's systems is designed around unearthing that value within people.
However, value is fragile. You need only look at it from another angle, and the mighty will become the tiny. The hideous, the beautiful.
It's a pity that, whether through apathy or inertia, people tend to not change viewpoints they are familiar with.
And so, people live on their immutable value. Were those values to crumble away, they would crumble alongside them.
Exactly.
When did despair begin to blossom in you? Was it when Milliarium left the home territories? Or even earlier, after the passing of your Familiar?
...Cassia?
I'm merely grateful that you came to see me today.
You reminded me that I still have things to do, and time runs short.
I'm surprised you allowed such pessimism to spread in your city, Clementia.
'The Death of Life,' a prehistoric diary collated and published by Avitus. The year it was released, it proved to be incredibly popular among the citizens.
They wanted a peek into an unfamiliar life. A thirst born from boredom and idiocy.
What you just said would be enough to trigger controversy in any nation on the land, Gladiia.
A tiny piece that implies exhaustion and despair with every line. It tells the story of how a once glorious civilization hit a dead end far more vividly than any raw data.
And in today's Ægir, can it really be considered an 'unfamiliar life'?
Ægir may be the prehistoric civilization's heir, but that does not require its subservience. We need not condone the rise of... 'exhaustion and despair.'
Ægir stands unwavering due to its unbreakable rationality.
It is our rationality that allows us to be aware of our possible destruction, perhaps even one of all humanity.
Even those who have fallen, detestable as they are, at times make choices based on long-term examination and cautious reflection.
You have personally executed no small number of cultists in your days in the Legions.
Just as you had to personally execute any Hunters who ran wild. You and I are both burdened with heavy duties that we cannot rely on sentimentality.
Was it this rationality that led you to hastily throw the Hunters into the battle for the Waterway Program, despite their recent return?
Kal'tsit...
I invite the two of you to look at the beacon tower behind us. It was once an important component of the interstellar expedition our ancestors planned for.
Within the tower lie countless nanomachines. The first Ægir city to break through the electron cloud would have seeded them throughout the sea of stars.
They would self-proliferate, self-repair, and connect together into an ever bright road network. With this, our fleets would have been able to jump freely between the stars.
After the outbreak of the Seaborn crisis, Ægir turned its eyes from the stars, and the bio-energy system requiring support from a dozen or so cities was cut off.
The nanomachines were incompatible with other energy sources, so after losing their specialized source, they all 'died' within that tower.
But our colleagues from the Academy of Technology have helped alter this tower to serve us in a different manner, by lighting our paths.
Since Milliarium left the home territories, it has been continuously marking Seaborn nests. Huge volumes of data are now compiled within that tower's control center.
With the precise locations of these Seaborn nests, our Class IV weapons can destroy the primary nest in any targeted area of ocean, driving away the occupying Seaborn.
This will clear a path of pure water leading back to the home territories. One that can support a Waterway our main forces can pass through.
Perhaps the Seaborn will eventually regroup, but at the very least, their accumulated nutrients will rot away in their absence, and any re-established nests will bear no fruit.
You are lecturing us on the core concept of the 'Waterway Program,' Madame Consul.
Yes I am, Doctor.
The Abyssal Hunters only managed to save twenty-six lives in yesterday's battle. The Seaborn were clearly forewarned.
Between the oceanic interior and the continental shelf, this city has been fighting them for far too long. They are now adapting to Class III weapons at a much faster rate.
Nest 37's position is of particular import. It strangles the throat of the Waterway. Abandoning it would mean stumbling at the final hurdle.
This is our final battle before the Waterway can be opened. We must gather all the force we can bear as part of the program. The Abyssal Hunters are no exception.
Even if they have only just arrived home.
Can Ægir ensure their safe return?
New combat teams are being formed as we speak. They will confront the Seaborn directly, and create an opportunity for the Hunters to enter the nest and deploy the beacon.
But, as you know, these are merely routine tactics. I am... unable to offer a guarantee.
In that case...
I know what you are worried about. Be at ease.
The Hunters are responsible for their own safety, as well as the risks they face.
As this is a joint decision by you two Chief Designers of Warfare, I am unopposed, but I will not withdraw my objections.
Gladiia...
An unbecoming sentimentality bubbles up in Clementia's throat.
She wants to convey her guilt. Her remorse. Her regret.
She wants to give Gladiia one last hug, just like how she welcomed her back on her return.
But she cannot.
Gladiia.
Advisor Blandus has made preparations for your organic regeneration surgery. You should make your own before the procedure.
Doña Irene!
Jordi.
You were gone for a whole day...
I was investigating a few things with the Sea Patrol. I also went on a trip into the ocean. I didn't enjoy it very much, so don't ask.
Alright then.
What about you? What have you been up to?
It's not unusual to feel some discomfort. Cheer up, Jordi, this is your homeland.
(Chuckles wryly)
Judging by your expression, you've already studied Breogan's data?
The young Ægir somehow nods his head while also shaking it.
I'm not 100% sure, but there seems to be no real relation between me and that great naval architect.
What does that matter?
Uncle Thiago hoped I'd leave Gran Faro, leave Iberia, but he never told me where to go...
I'm Ægir, so Ægir is where I should be.
But people need some sort of concrete connection, even just a name or some notes, to truly trace their family history.
Avitus said that even though the Seaborn crisis has been raging for over two hundred years, Ægir still has a hundred or more cities comparable in size to Milliarium.
If I'm not Breogan's descendant, then which city did my grandparents live in before they became Islanders? Or was it one of the ones that's gone now?
What kind of work did they do? How did they live? In the end, who were they?
Sorry, you don't need to hear all this. You've just arrived here yourself, but you're already assisting the Sea Patrol with their investigation. While I'm just...
You've got nerves of steel.
I'm just an Iberian who can't do a thing here.

Dr. Kal'tsit is in charge of negotiations with the leader of this city, and the Hunters have dived right back into their familiar battle. While my lantern and handcannon aren't especially helpful right now.
Ægir will permit a Liberi to look around its warships and ports, but will not allow her to join the Legions and help fight against the Seaborn. I can't understand that sort of war.
Down here in the depths, all I can do is investigate any abnormal activity within the city. In case it could jeopardize the cooperation between Iberia and Ægir.
Why are you telling me all this?
My hometown was destroyed in a Catastrophe, Jordi. I don't have many memories of it.
The scripture I carry is the only connection with it I have left. It saved my life, and in doing became my guide, my faith.

But when I saw the Inquisition's dungeons, climbed aboard the Stultifera Navis, witnessed my mentor's sacrifice... Only then did I understand what faith was. Accepted its weaknesses. Found its meaning.
You mean...
I don't have nerves of steel, Jordi. I get lost and confused, too.
It's just that, more often than not, that confusion becomes a specific issue you have to resolve. One after another, as quickly as you can.
Arresting the cultists. Taking back the lighthouse invaded by the tides. Finding a lost warship out at sea. Aiding an ally in investigating a missing data scientist.
The 'connection' you're looking for can be anything, whether it's a hometown or faith. Keep looking. You'll find it. It may not be quick or easy, but you'll find it.
Can I really do it?
Can you not?
You helped Iberia relight its final lighthouse, and saved three Abyssal Hunters. Didn't Laurentina tell you that that's enough to get a commendation from the consul herself?
Whether it's as an Ægir or an Inquisition clerk, there are a lot of things you can do.
Praefecta!
You're back to the city. But... From the look on your face, I take it the investigation didn't go so well, did it?
Lucilla?
Forgive me, I need to report on my progress to Consul Clementia. I'm not at liberty to divulge any details.
A-Actually, I was looking for you.
There's something I should've remembered back when you were explaining the situation to us. Nothing major, honestly, and it might not even help.
But I felt like I had to tell you!
...Go on.
You're the last researcher left.
I'm really, really sorry; can you give me one more hour?
Caulerpa archaeosphaeridium, Hypnea fenestralis, Halymenia fimbriatus, Palmaria purpureus, Sargassum pennata... There's not too many left. I'll finish cataloging them real quick!
No need to rush, Tullia. I'm just here for a routine inspection.
You know who I am?
Mhm. We were in the same batch before the transfer assessment by the Institute of Development Planning. You might not remember me, though.
Are you preparing to transport these seaweeds?
Th-This doesn't violate any protocols, does it?!
Calm down. This building's handover procedures are already complete. If you leave the samples and cultivation equipment behind, you'll end up having to pack them away in bulk.
Thanks for the warning.
But with all these seaweeds, how could you possibly take them all away at once?
Now that it's close to land, Milliarium will be undergoing targeted reformation. The city won't have the space or the resources to breed seaweeds.
Yeah, I know.
I'll take as much as I can. I'll think of something, some way that won't inconvenience anyone.
But the base by the volcano is already...
That was once Ægir's biggest endangered plants reserve. I saw it get swallowed up by the Seaborn.
These primitive seaweeds are some of the simplest, yet most tenacious lifeforms in the entire ocean.
Whether in bright shallow seas, the dark abyss, the flat sea floor, or cracks in the cliffs, they stretch out as best they can.
And they're as old as the ocean itself, pretty much this planet's living fossils.
I'm no legionnaire, and I can't go fight the Seaborn. These remaining seaweeds won't help us solve the Seaborn problem, either.
But I can't just let them wither away in here.
...You really love your work, huh Tullia?
Um, don't you?
Ah, my Little Handy sent over a loading module. Sorry, but I can't stay and chat.
Do you need any help?
No thanks, I'm fine.
When did that happen?
The day before the Institute of Ecological Art relocated.
I don't know what happened to her after that, and I don't know if she thinks Milliarium abandoned the volcanic power station and the plants reserve...
But I can't imagine her doing anything bad because of it... because she's never abandoned anything herself.
Tullia's residence had quite a few hydroponic tanks, but they'd been empty for a while...
What you just told me might actually be significant, Lucilla.
But if I may be so bold, why do you care so much about her affairs?
From what I've heard, you could hardly be considered friends.
She...
She has something I don't.
Okay, I've said what I had to say. I'll be getting back to work now, Praefecta.
There's just been more and more happening lately. The maintenance required for the city unit framework has multiplied several-fold, and I'm running out of ink as it is.
Little Handy.
Praefecta Secunda.
Obtain data on the city of Milliarium from the Institute of Facility Management, right away. Retrieve all locations suitable for enclosed hydroponics.
Also, contact Irene.
Tullia may have left other clues within the city. Ask her to meet me as soon as possible.
Reassessing. Biological safety of operating theater now at permitted levels.
Surgery may begin at any time.
Hunters, just as before, you will be kept in a deep sleep during the automated surgery.
I will also be manually repairing your outdated internal auto-adaptive junctions.
This room filled to the brim with specimens, and these lab tables are all rather familiar. How long has it been since we last laid down in such an environment?
Five years, Laurentina.
I should really be waiting for your medulla replacement surgery to finish and your cell-Originium assimilation rate to stabilize before I handle any of your Hunter-related issues.
But time is short, and I have no choice but to restore you to stable condition to the best of my abilities.
My apologies, Laurentina.
Sentimental as always.
Ah, right, as usual...
A candle will be lit by your side, so our little mermaid won't look so pale while she sleeps. I've asked my staff to prepare it for you.
I knew you'd remember.
Please maintain a comfortable position and stay still while the regen gel fills.
This isn't so different from back then. Except there's a lot less Hunters now.
Clear a trench of scum, close your eyes, listen to some advisor prattle on, then sink into the regen gel and take a nap.
And once you wake up, all the nauseating smells left on your body will be gone without a trace.
While you were in the adverse, fickle environments of the land for long periods without care, your bodies became extremely unstable.
The auto-adaptive junction can monitor your Seaborn genes, but five years of wear can render even the most precise instruments dull.
Moreover, your immune systems have also fallen into disorder thanks to their long struggle against the Seaborn genes.
I will do my best to restore you all to peak condition, but anything lost during those five years is out of my hands.
Okay.
Gladiia, our reunion at the tribunal was less than cordial, so I would have liked for us to consider this our first meeting since your return.
But you haven't said a word since we started.
Is there anything else you need to tell us in regards to the surgery, Blandus?
If not, then save it for after the procedure. We can discuss the changes we've felt over the past five years, as well as the changes you've felt.
Please stay still. Anesthetic procedures will begin in ten seconds.
Well then. Sweet dreams, Hunters.
Advisor Blandus, some things have come up that need your reconfirmation.
Did I not say that once the operating theater doors have closed, I would be mending the Hunters' auto-adaptive junctions and must not be disturbed?
Central Control has discovered some abnormalities. You should personally check them over, just to be safe.
...Hold on then. I'll get the door.
The monitoring units in the dome were performing an emergency scan. We noticed a concentration of algal bio-signatures here.
Thanks for informing me.
Not a problem.
Since you're already participating in Tullia's case, there was no reason to cut you out partway.
A place where the dome and the ground meet... This former experimental field for road-side coral.
Though it can't compare to the specialized setups at the Institute of Ecological Art, it's much better for seaweed breeding than small-scale hydroponic tanks in a room.
Whether it be in her nature, or out of an ulterior motive, Tullia did not apply for authorization to use this space. She found it on her own, without telling anyone.
Tullia...
We can't say for sure if there is a Church hideout here, or if Tullia is still within... In short, stay alert.
Team 1 has already formed a defensive perimeter around the neighborhood, and will monitor the roads. Irene, go with Team 2. Enter via surface roads.
As for the roads connecting the dome and the mezzanine, leave them to me.
Got it.
Move out.
Who's there?
Have you found something, Praefecta Secunda? Do you need reinforcements?
Somebody blocks her path. Or more precisely, prevents her from leaving.
The one in her way lurks in the shadows formed by the angles of the buildings, their entire body blending into the darkness save their eyes. Eyes that Secunda are all too familiar with.
...Stick to the plan.
Secunda cuts her comms.
...It's been a while, 'Magister.'
Blandus, the weapons technology advisor for the Waterway Program, and an expert in Class IV weapon R&D, correct?
...You're not from the lab.
No sudden moves. If the bristles of this longbeard combtooth grass pierce your skin, you'll quickly fall into a tormented dream, where you'll sense your consciousness being slowly torn apart.
The variety used for euthanasia has been bred to be milder, but wild ones are still plenty cruel.
You may look half-dead, but I know for a fact that you're the sort who really cares how they die.
Church of the Deep?
Don't you dare touch the anesthetized Hunters!
What an odd fellow. Even as your life is threatened, you think of them first?
You may care for the Hunters, but you've never trusted the Abyssal Hunter project itself. You question its rationale, its damage to the nature of humanity, and all the sacrifice it requires.
You took part in it, but only so you could personally control all the risks entailed.
You experienced its demise firsthand; saw the extensive project, interlinked with so many others, come to a hasty end after an uncertain failure.
So I doubt you'd want to send these survivors, freshly returned to Ægir, back onto the battlefield.
No, you don't understand. What I do is only to protect them from further harm.
So why not halt the procedure? Stop them from joining the battle for the Waterway Program?
The surgery is fully automated, I can't just stop it.
Sever the oxygen pipeline below the operating table, and it'll cut the procedure short. Don't think we don't know that.
If you won't do it, we can do it for you.
After you finish 'handling' the Hunters, Advisor, come take a walk with us. Explain the principles behind the Class IV weapons, as well as how to shut them down.
Stop! If the operation is forcibly terminated, the Hunters will be irreversibly harmed—
Scum.
The 'researcher' stops in his tracks. He dares not step forward, yet dares not move back, for a lance has pierced his torso, his blood dripping down its shaft.
If you do not wish me to ram this straight through your chest, let go of your hand.
What are you doing here?
Don't ask pointless questions, Secunda.
You're investigating Tullia, too. Did you find anything?
She is simply an ordinary data scientist who got caught up in this. She discovered something she shouldn't have. Attempted to warn the authorities, but failed.
You were too slow. What she encountered has nothing to do with her past.

The battle outside the city, the abnormalities within, the Sea Patrol staying uninvolved with the Waterway Program. You've noticed many oddities, yet at the same time, your attention was so easily diverted.
You mean... Cassia?
Precisely.
You were once so focused, and keen as well.
So the day you decided to leave the Abyssal Hunter Lab to join the Sea Patrol, I believed you had reflected upon yourself and chosen a more suitable path.
It seems that was not the only time you have allowed emotion to cloud your decisions.
...Aside from your clothes, you haven't changed a bit in five years. Especially not how you talk.
Whether as a researcher or a Hunter, you'd never refuse to hear a student's questions, but would refuse to answer if you thought the question itself was wrong.
There are no worthless answers. Their worth lies in the questions.
Questions are the most effective means of tempering your thoughts, and your questions were always most pointed, Secunda.
Then there should be no fault with my original question. What are you doing here, Ulpianus?
This is the scene of a Sea Patrol investigation. A fugitive Abyssal Hunter like you should be laying down his anchor and making clear his position.
Gladiia and the others have proven an effective force in the battle for the Waterway Program, while despite also surviving, you chose to return to Milliarium like this.
...That's more than enough grounds to attack you.
Their return will only make the situation more volatile. Even through Tullia's case, it's clear that the so-called 'Waterway Program' is riddled with issues.
Ægir has learned nothing from the failure of the Abyssal Hunter project.
What are you going to do?
Prevent this nation from sliding into the abyss.
I'd say you're looking awfully like our enemy now, though.
Doubt me as you will, Secunda.
(Shrill crying)
What's happening in there?
A ordinary Ægir, doing all she can.
But Ægir will no longer show her any respect.
Who're you—?
Irene...
Ulpianus?
Nobody responds. The Hunter has already faded into the shadows.
What the—?! Why aren't you asleep?!
I'm not the only one.
Hello. I suggest moving the hand carrying that whatever-grass away from the advisor's neck.

You—
Arghhh—!
A fair hand tightly grips the plant held by the 'researcher,' its glistening tentacles struggling fiercely as they are nipped off one by one.
I told you to move it away.
The real researchers are in the instrument recycling room, probably injected with paralytics. They won't wake up for a while.
I also took care of the goons in the corridor.
Did you leave any alive for questioning?
They won't die before the Sea Patrol takes them away, at least.
Gladiia, Laurentina, Skadi...
Blandus stands in the center of the operating theater. The Hunters step towards him, over the intruders lying flat on the ground.
They do not lower their weapons.
Not going to explain the trick you played with the regen gel, advisor?
*sigh* My dearest Blandus, just to be clear, we are no fortune tellers.
Swordfish thought something was off from the start.
We've undergone hundreds of surgeries. We've probably spent more time sleeping in gels than in our beds.
You should know that even if the operation failed to restore us to our full potential, we would still have returned to the battlefield.
As I told Ulpianus when he came to me, all I wanted was for the participants of this program to live like normal people again someday.
I wished for my fellow people, who'd come from all walks of life, to have the chance to finally return to their former lives.
Skadi could continue her life of wandering, hammering away at complex lighting arrays while taking pictures of the seabed nobody else would understand.
Laurentina's skills could let her become the next shining star of the Institute of Physical Art.
We could watch the graceful stars projected across the dome, while she dances alongside the meteors in the Sophontheatrum.
Or perhaps we would find her works on the street corners of each and every city.
Flavius, the seaweed breeding technician. Nona, the songwriter. Lunia, the exotic organism researcher. And Celsus, the youngest ever scientific exploration fleet commander.
We both still remember the names of all these Hunters, Gladiia. We've both had our fill of sacrifice.
This is far from the first time I've heard this little cliché of yours.
Your feebleness and stupidity are already enough to earn you a death by my lance.
Luckily for you, those Church scum arrived at just the right time to save your hide.
Are you saying their actions have coincidentally proven my innocence?
That's not what I wanted, Gladiia.
What I must do will not change.
Your innocence is of little relevance.
My point was that by choosing to expose themselves now, risking everything to stop us Hunters from rejoining the battle, and even holding an advisor hostage...
All it shows is how effective the deployment of the Waterway Program has been.
Blandus, weapons technology advisor of the Waterway Program. Abandon your little tricks, complete our surgery, then return to your original post.
Clementia's orders are that once Nest 37 has been successfully marked, the Class IV weapons are to immediately strike against all nests by the Waterway.
The Church is grasping at straws. As one can surmise from what those dregs were talking about earlier, they will not stop.
The Abyssal Hunter project no longer exists, Blandus. If sentimentality clouds your mind and renders you feeble, tear it out. Forget it.
Gladiia.
You're the same as ever. No, even stricter with yourself.
What I ask of myself has no bearing here.
Just yesterday, out of two entire fleets, we were only able to save twenty-six people from the Seaborn encirclement.
Before we entered this old lab, I had just learned that those twenty-six legionnaires had already applied for positions in the newly-drawn army.
War is never-ending, and no soldier can tear themselves away from the battlefield. That's all it is. Nothing more.
...Seaborn.
Where dome and ground meet, an entire wall is covered in remodeled hydroponic tanks. Various species of seaweed grow wildly, and several large clusters have already begun to grow out of their tanks.
Separated by a transparent wall, they face the gloomy seawater.
(Muffled cry)
A lone Seaborn wanders about, like a lost spirit.
Its tentacles are tightly wound around a transparent container, and the rest of its body shakes and sways as it drags itself towards the tanks.
(This clumsy posture...)

(As though it's unaccustomed to its body...)
Are you...
Tullia?
Tu—lli—a...
You can still speak. You still have your humanity. Tell me, what happened to you?
Left... and returned...
Data. Someone used... Waterway, signal...
I told, her. But she...
Told who?
Ocean... Fall into—ocean.
City left. You. They're still... here...
*whimper* So far...
Give her some space, Irene. The Seaborn cells have completely transformed her body. Her mind is near the point of no return.
We've handled similar transformations. This stage in Seaborn degeneration is extremely unstable. It's far beyond the point where we can provide palliative care.
You sure took your time.
I ran into something.
We've been misled.
Someone deliberately engendered suspicions regarding the scientist's past and her motives for joining the Waterway Program. It's led the investigation astray.
You mean that woman from the Institute of Facility Management.
That's right. Cassia. Once we're finished here, the Sea Patrol will inform the Institute of Facility Management and arrest her.
Tullia noticed occasional abnormalities in the signaling system and told a friend, possibly Cassia. She exposed herself to the Church.
She'd have been chased towards the fringes of the city, where she fell into a drain and was carried out to sea by the city's circulatory system.
Do you remember how we found pieces of biological tissue on our excursion outside? Crossing between two anchorage points without equipment is far beyond the limits of the human form.
So in her desperation to return to the city...
She ate that strange flesh.
(Muffled, indistinct murmuring)
What's that she's holding?
Plant nutrient fluid.
Her consciousness should be gone by now. Don't tell me she returned to the city just for these... seaweeds?
'These primitive seaweeds are some of the simplest, yet most tenacious lifeforms in the entire ocean.'
'Whether in bright shallow seas, the dark abyss, the flat sea floor, or cracks in the cliffs, they stretch out as best they can.'
'And they're as old as the ocean itself, pretty much this planet's living fossils.'
(Violent shudder)
(Pained wail)

The Seaborn suddenly stops, as though it has just noticed something. Its tentacles recklessly thrash about, and the transparent container tumbles from its grasp, spilling nutrient fluid all over the floor.
Bio-monitor readings increasing rapidly! Her transformation is accelerating!
Why did she suddenly stop? Did something happen?
Irene walks in front of the Seaborn.
As she passes by the glass of the hydroponic tanks, she sees her own face.
Yes. I believe... she saw what she had become.
...A data scientist, yet also a woman as simple as the seaweeds she cultivates.
She chose to stay, or perhaps knew that following the Institute of Ecological Art to another city would not truly have helped these seaweeds as they gradually lose their place out in the wild ocean.
But now... she has become one of the pollutants threatening them herself.
Irene closes her eyes.
She never knew this Ægir woman who loved seaweeds. She's only ever seen her image in the Little Handy. And now, before their first meeting, she's already become the Seaborn she sees before her.
Whether by rapier or handcannon, she should end its life.
I believe she can no longer answer our questions.
It seems you're familiar with this sort of thing.
I once heard a Seaborn play a broken song aboard a ship.
I apologize for underestimating you earlier, Irene.

The land and the sea are connected. You may not have experienced the frontlines of the Seaborn conflict, but you've witnessed both what has changed and what has not, equal in their weight and cruelty.
But for now, let me handle this.
She is an Ægir.
Irene sees Secunda walk past her side, stopping in front of the Seaborn.
The expressionless Praefecta places her shield between the Seaborn and the wall covered in tanks, then raises her weapon at the once-woman.
(Wails)
Her journey ended♪
Her home close at hand♪
This song...
She chose this place to sleep.
The Seaborn collapses to the floor. The commander's shield blocks its spurting bodily fluids from entering the hydroponic tanks.
The seaweeds sway, quietly respirating.

lines.st: Voice Log #33
(Unknown language, unknown time)
The stargate's remodeling plan is an apparent success, and we have re-established a connection with Talos. A two-year backlog of information has been flooding in, but it is bare of any good tidings.
First it was the observatory stations on the asteroid belt, then the entire system's interstellar hub. Finally, the cruise fleets orbiting hospitable planets also fell into silence.
Even the ancient colonial fleet turned into a nomadic museum was not spared the same fate.
Along with these shocking revelations came similarly unexpected data left behind by Valeria. Her notes resembled her very being: long-winded and inexhaustible.

However, she seemed to have spent her last days repeatedly poring over literature and history alongside the technical documents. Studying how these great men died, what their last words and epitaphs were...
Like a child on the day before their coming-of-age ceremony, rifling through their parent's wardrobe in a frantic search for attire dignified enough for an adult.
Did she find her own life inadequate? Insufficiently weighty?
No, she left practically all that she had at the stargate. If she had lived in the past, wouldn't her achievements have been inserted into the annals of history?
But now it seemed nothing more than a narrow door opened between two tiny locked rooms in the dark. I fear it won't be long before the door is shut once more.
In the end, the history of humanity has exceeded the bounds of humanity itself, hah.

lines.n: 'Little Handy' from the Institute of Facility Management
lines.x: Reading error occurred (2).

lines.n: 'Little Handy' from the Institute of Facility Management
lines.x: Run fault diagnostics?
Do you need my help, Mr. Avitus?
Oh, no. I was merely observing this abandoned Little Handy.
Abandoned...? By who?
Don't worry, that's not important. What brings you here, Jordi?
I'm hoping to find something to do. We're clearly in dire straits, and I'm sure this city is full of problems in need of solving. They just won't reveal themselves to me.
Generally speaking, in that situation you should go to the Institute of Development Planning for an assessment.
However, if you were looking for me specifically, I'll try my best to help you out.
You're a highly experienced and respected scholar, after all, and many in the city call you Magister.
Magister? My last work was 'The Death of Life,' a book with no educational value whatsoever.
I-I read it, actually. Although I couldn't understand much, with so many cryptic words. Like starship, stargate... it seemed like from another world.
'Another world.' Hah, you're right about that.
But I find myself undeserving of the title Magister. Often times I find myself lost in confusion.
Jordi, do you remember when I told you that blood ties were unimportant?
I do, but I already...
I may have been a bit misleading. While we indeed care little for blood relations, 'relationships' themselves are an excellent lead to follow.
When one is lost, one ought to find a connection to reality—be it a life or an ideal—and seek a way out.
Otherwise you'll end up like me, sinking into an extended questioning of the value of your own existence.
If I may be so bold, can I ask whatyour own 'relationship' is...?
My Familiar.
How, exactly...?
She shared the same thoughts and goals, and would enrich both my spirit and my ideas. I loved her as ardently as I loved Ægir, as well as everything we've done for it.
I live a life surrounded by mountains of prehistoric material written in their strange script and incomprehensible grammar... I enjoy the process of translating it into Ægirian text.
That was how we met, and I still remember how she'd throw herself into her work... Her eyebrows seemed to dance whenever she talked about something of interest to her.
And when I told her so, in those exact words, she actually went to the Institute of Physical Art to find out if eyebrows could dance for real.
What a strange form of art... Did they manage it?
Strictly speaking, no. But some of their researchers were inspired to later develop the technology to make hair dance on its own.
We've experienced the catastrophe that silenced the entire sea, and the Seaborn continue to threaten our existence, but we remain committed to exploring science, art, and our vision for the future.

Lava bridges that pump power from the planet's mantle, aquatic artery hubs that transform currents into fountains, artificial suns illuminating the depths of the oceanic abyss, and ecological bases filling the barren seafloor with life...
We persist in transforming what the Predecessors lost into mystical megastructures. We need only a spark to spur on countless inventions.
One hundred fifty years is truly too short.
One hundred and fifty years?
Yes, the average lifespan of an Ægir.
'Short,' you say...
The more dangerous the era, the more valuable our creations become.
We firmly believed in living fulfilling lives, and would gladly have gone to our end without regrets as we passed down the fire of civilization.
We planned an educational system we believed was better than the Institute of Incubation.
Once our child was born via the incubator, we would raise them ourselves. They would possess a surging spirit and fresh ideologies, and become part of a more creative generation.
It is unusual to raise children personally, but we had hoped to pass on the values we treasured from the fulfilling lives we'd lived.
So, what happened to her?
Dead.
Remember the Predecessor mentioned in 'The Death of Life'?
He took shelter in an unknown refuge as he watched the sky pressing down on the earth every day. He already had a premonition of his own end.
There was nothing he could do but calmly welcome its advent.
But that's someone else's life, a life far too distant from ours.
Is it, though? Are we not currently going through the same things?
Give it fifty years, maybe a hundred, and even the vast ocean may no longer hold a single drop of pure water.
The Seaborn have unwittingly overrun the ocean faster than we can create, devouring what we were once proud of, turning 'survival' into our only priority.
I watched as she was engulfed by despair. One day, she calmly gave up on our idea of raising our child ourselves.
After all, everything we have inherited would only be lost in an era predestined to perish.
Five years ago, the Abyssal Hunters went to the lair of the Firstborn, and no one returned alive.
Soon after, she got sick, and died in depression. Our son was successfully born in the incubator, but I sent him to the Institute of Incubation.
This was also when I began questioning the meaning of my own existence.

My apologies, Jordi. I was supposed to be providing you guidance, wasn't I? I've already severed my relationship with the real world, but perhaps you have someone or something you still have ties with?
But I gave up chasing Breogan long ago. I can't waste even more time chasing an unreachable shadow.
Maybe... Maybe I could delve into the notes he left, and learn how he developed his tech. And not just because I might be his descendant.
...But because you also have talent and dreams in that field.
You can try the beacon tower, then. Breogan was involved in its reconstruction, so if you're familiar with his handiwork, perhaps you could help them out.
Every Ægir leaves their own quirks in their writings, theories, or technologies, so sometimes all you need to sort out a trickier part is someone more familiar with the author.
Thank you, Mr. Avitus!
Advisor Blandus? Are you alright?
Of course, of course.
The cultists failed to stop me from performing the surgery on the Hunters.
I'm asking about your physical condition.
Thanks, Secunda. I see you're still—
Given your strange aversion to medical aid, someone has to confirm you're alright.
Although judging from your condition, I'm guessing I'm not the first to try sending you to the hospital.
Perhaps the Waterway Program is too overwhelming. I'm something of a doctor myself, so I do know how to keep myself healthy—healthy enough to not slow down the program, anyway.
You know, you should take a look at the plant the cultists were using to kill people. It's difficult to imagine someone at the Institute of Ecological Art using it as paint.
Well, seeing how you're not in any immediate danger, I'll need you to cooperate with my investigation.
I saw Ulpianus. Recently.
I see you have too.
Only briefly, at the Sophontheatrum.
And you didn't report this?
Catching a momentary glimpse of a familiar figure is hardly worth reporting.
I'm not joking around, Advisor Blandus.
The data scientist's case has been confirmed to be a murder by the cultists, and it may be related to the assault on the lab.
Ulpianus seemed to know the victim well, and openly expressed his distrust of Ægir.
He's always been like that. More action than words, and ever unwilling to reveal his reasons.
His unique style doesn't explain all the doubts about him. I still need to check if he's linked to several Church cases.
*sigh* Secunda, do you really lack any other leads or suspects more worthy of investigation?
Or I should ask, is this obsession with Ulpianus merely your own whim?
*sigh*
Secunda, I'm the one who rejected your application back then.
What did you just say?
I said, it was me who rejected your application to volunteer to become an Abyssal Hunter.
Ulpianus concurred, but I was the one who officially rejected you.
...Why?
I saw your documents from the Institute of Development Planning. You'd written 'genetic research' in the box asking for your aspirations.
You only had to look at another file to see that becoming a Hunter was one another of my aspirations.
We considered you extremely talented, even among the participants of the Abyssal Hunter project.
During our technical discussions, you were the only one to ask Ulpianus ten questions in a row and get answers for all of them. The highest record for a freshman researcher.
The early transformation techniques were dangerous and unreliable. We were always searching for a better integration method. It was ultimately your theories that advanced the program's technology.
I considered it an honor back then, at least.
But the program itself is nothing to be proud of, Secunda.
The concept was born nearly two centuries ago, a far cry from the average citizen's impression today. It was only twenty-some years ago that Ulpianus made it into a reality.
In the hundred plus years before that, it was never afforded any systematic scrutiny or research. Only endless debates.
To exploit our countrymen and integrate the flesh of other species into ourselves to save our humanity was tantamount to toppling the very foundations of Ægir.
But Ulpianus is a decisive man. He can abandon his research, ideals, emotions, and even his humanity without a shred of hesitation. The sole principle he abides by is 'To benefit Ægir.'
And perhaps he will save Ægir someday, but our country should not be founded upon boundless self-sacrifice.
Ulpianus understands better than anyone that his very existence is actually his country's woe.
And so, 'The Abyssal Hunters do not need a second Ulpianus. Ægir should not have a second Ulpianus.'
'There should be no one in Ægir who wishes to become another.'
...I could practically hear him saying him.

I once joked to him how much better it would be if you were my student instead. Perhaps you could continue the research he and I used to work on: 'The evolution of Ægir cells.' Or perhaps you'd start a new field entirely.
You could have become one of the leading scientists of our time, with the opportunity to explore the primordial mysteries of all existence and lead us into the future.
Blandus, the two of you had no right.
I'm sorry, Secunda.
Did you start reminiscing about the old days just to tell me I shouldn't bear a grudge against Ulpianus?
I did it so you'd see him in a new light.
Being too eager to capture him will do neither of you any good.
Aren't there cultists ready and available for questioning? Don't forget your duties, Secunda.
Where are they?
The first floor. In a booth in the equipment administration room. Do you need me to show you the way?
I know where it is. The Sea Patrol will take over the detainment of the criminals.
I'm so sorry, but you can't go any further. We're currently—
Wait, didn't I see you at the tribunal?
I know who you are, the emissary from land. If you don't mind me asking, what exactly are your duties on this diplomatic mission?
The data scientist seemed to have completely transformed into a Seaborn by the time she was found.
It would be difficult to obtain any further information from her, other than the fact that this set of data seems related to the 'Waterway' and the 'Beacons.'
I've finished analyzing the contents: there are unusual data interactions between the beacon within the city and the ones deployed in the nests.
The signals come from many spots within the city, and I have already inspected the port hub and any possible ships.
The only remaining location is Lab C-22. One of the earliest Abyssal Hunter experimental research labs.

opts: You should be familiar with the place, then.

lines.x: However, it is far livelier here than I can remember it ever being.
Secunda has even deployed a garrison in the name of protecting Blandus.
A hindrance.

opts: I'll help draw their attention if needed.

lines.x: I can manage the Sea Patrol for now.
You should focus on the toughest target.
You would be... the Doctor of Rhodes Island, yes?

opts: As you say.
In the flesh.

lines.x: You say your institution helped diagnose and treat Oripathy in our Hunters, and now you hope to assist me with their organic regeneration surgery?

opts: Absolutely.
That's exactly what I'm here for.

lines.x: Then you can leave with peace of mind.
The Church's assault here was just a minor episode, and the Hunters have already returned to their hibernation.
I'll be constantly monitoring their physical condition, so your gaze—no matter how vigilant it may be—will be of little use in their recovery.

opts: I see the Hunters were telling the truth.
You really have a terrible sense of humor.

lines.x: ...I see they have developed a deep friendship with you while wandering about on land.
Insist all you want, it doesn't change the fact that you will be of no help during the operation.
I mean no offense.
This place was just recently raided, so please return to where you came from, for your own safety.
Blandus no longer pays you any attention, his gaze falling back onto the holographic projections before him.
Through those squares you can clearly see Gladiia, Skadi, and Laurentina deep asleep in a transparent gel.

You didn't manage to get a full grasp of Ægir's writing system before coming here. After days of fumbling your way through the city, you can still only recognize about half the symbols shown in the visuals.
But you observe a more important phenomenon within them:
Whenever Blandus selects an image, it would emit a faint beep.
That is to say, every chosen image generates a corresponding beep on the monitoring unit.

opts: Could I help arrange the visual display?

lines.x: Of course, of course.
I see you really want to stay. I won’t force you to leave, either. It seems you really do care for the Hunters, and I like people like you.
Little Handy, the Doctor is unfamiliar with the equipment in Ægir, so keep an eye on things.
Understood.
Doctor, as long as you don't touch anything you shouldn't, then—
The corridor's monitoring unit just beeped. Someone's looking in here.
Another beep for the experimental cabin in front and the corner further ahead... Hmm? There seems to be some sort of pattern.
Someone in the control room is rapidly switching the order of the holograms back and forth.
...Doctor? What are you trying to tell me?
This way, then.
The operation is complete, Doctor. Thank you for your... fast and furious cooperation.
The Hunters will need another ten minutes to awaken, but I guarantee the procedure went without a hitch.
So if you could now leave—

opts: The Hunters seem to be deep asleep.

lines.x: They were unable to get any proper rest on land, despite what I'm sure was the best possible accommodation from you and Rhodes Island.
Even now, they've just only arrived home, yet now must rush back to the frontlines.
A single operation and hibernation session is far from enough to restore them to full strength.

opts: It sounds like you tried your best.

lines.x: I wouldn't say so, even if I've fulfilled my duties. There is a fundamental difference between the two, after all.
I believe you mentioned your expertise in the field of Oripathy?
Could you tell me what it's like, to be a patient?

opts: They fight their fate for the rest of their lives.

lines.x: 'Fate'?

opts: It means an inevitable direction or end.

lines.x: Interesting. The ancient sages once discussed the concept of 'fate,' but it's been a long time since anyone has brought it up.
You see, Oripathy does not exist in the ocean, and most other diseases we managed to cure.
We all choose our own paths in life, and the trajectory of our civilization is ours to define.
For the longest time, we have forgotten what 'fate' even is.
Yet now, the ineradicable disease we've engraved into our Hunters' blood cannot be dealt with medically.
To borrow your concept, one may call such an problem 'fate.'
Each of the Hunters held their own dreams, their own lives. But now? They have no choice but to live alongside slaughter until they die themselves.
I've tried many methods to bring them back from this dead end, all to no avail.
Even now...

opts: Gladiia's awake.
Laurentina's awake.
Skadi's awake.

lines.x: They seem to be in a hurry.
...I should get going too.
It has been an honor to speak with you, Doctor. I hope you can take good care of them, if given the chance.
Sea Patrol. Here to interrogate me?
'Interrogate.' Ha. I never thought I'd have anything to do with that word.
You should have thought of that when you intruded on the lab.
What about before then? What makes you and I so different?
Why 'interrogate' me? What have I done to deserve being 'questioned'?
At times like these, I feel like our methods are too gentle.
According to our envoy from Iberia, they're more than happy to incorporate punishment into the process.
So if you're finished talking nonsense, state your identity.
I'm a former road-side coral breeder.
Motive?
You ever paid close attention to the coral by the road, Patroller?
We hoped every cluster on every street would take on a different form or color, even different capabilities.
Transport tubes pass through the cavity-filled cluster on the east side of the Sophontheatrum, and under the glimmering tubes, the polyps glimmer back. A patchwork of dots of light...
Like a nebula in miniature.
Then there's the ones around the port—
As it happens, I have been paying close attention to some road-side coral recently. Specifically, the ones growing in your experimental field.
Where an innocent data scientist died.
Her dreams, her obsession, her love for her seaweeds... were as beautiful as the coral you speak of.
But you murdered her.
You asked a good question earlier. What makes you and her so different?
So why are you still alive? What did she do to deserve death?
She found...
Found what? Speak up.
Or are you implying I should be asking Cassia this question?
You...
What is the Church planning against the Waterway Program?
Will you be attacking the beacon, or the weapons?
Speak.
*sigh* Church this, cultist that.
I'm still not used to being called names like that. You sure they're not just convenient excuses for you to arrest people?
This so-called 'Church' and its 'cultists.' What part of them is 'holy,' exactly?
I don't worship any kind of imaginary entity. I just agree with how Cassia sees things.
From the despair engulfing Ægir, she can see this nation's fragile nature.
People place too much importance on the value of things, but are unwilling to recognize how fickle these values really are. They've become worshipers at their altar.
But now, those values are crumbling away. Before Ægir falls completely into despair, someone must sound the alarm—
Perii! You're just trying to loosen my lips!
You won't get another word out of me. If you're feeling impatient, go ahead, scan my brainwaves. You won't get anything out of that either—
Questioning you is the last respect I'll give you as a human being, scumbag!
Maybe you believe yourself innocent, and your actions free of conspiracy. But you will tell me everything I want to know.
Now speak.
Captain.
It's almost time for us to leave, Skadi.
I know it's urgent.
The frontline is barely holding ground against the Seaborn. The loss of two entire fleets is really hitting us hard now.
According to their most recent missive, Legions VIII and X failed to regroup into formation, and they are now mired in a bitter struggle.
If the beacon cannot be deployed at the designated position within the nest, and the deadlock continues... it will mean the failure of the Waterway Program.
Understood.
The Legion fleets are about to launch an offensive across the entire front, at all costs. All to attract the Seaborn's attention. As for us—
Squeeze through the battlefield, infiltrate the nest, and deploy the beacon.
Correct. How would you rate your current condition, Skadi?
I got some good sleep, at least. My first dreamless sleep since our last campaign.
The situation in the city is getting more restive... Do you know where the Doctor has gone?
(Shakes head)
I know you want me to keep the peace and protect the Doctor, Captain... You want me to stay.
To be honest, I am conflicted.
Cleansing the sea of its dregs is our duty, but I am also responsible for your safety.
Have there been any signs of Ishar'mla stirring since we returned to sea?
If it has, it's been real quiet.
If it speaks up, I'll shut it up.
Actually, I've got something to say to you, Captain.
We've never been this cautious in our work before. We had our ways of handling this sort of thing.
If the Church's god—the one the scum call 'Ishar'mla'—really is inside me, then if I ever go out of control, I want you to execute me on the spot. Simple as that.
My heart's right here. I trust you won't miss.
Skadi—
I'm not finished.
If you or Shark start to fall under their influence in battle before I do, I won't hesitate either.
What I was going to say is that, with war close at hand, I can imagine nothing more reassuring to hear.
Let's go.
Miss Laurentina.
No need for that dreary honorific, little bird. We're not strangers, are we?
...Laurentina.
Considering how long we've been in this city, I'm surprised this is the first time we've run into one another. This is sad.
I've always wanted to take our brave little warrior out on a real hunt in the depths. Sadly, the nest we're headed to is too far and too deep. No way to bring you along.
It's alright. Even if I had the option, I'd stay in Milliarium regardless.
Oh?
