But you stand here now, calmly explaining how you pushed Ægir into the abyss.
I've seen images of you. A gentle, meditative observer who has the habit of constantly pushing his large glasses back up his nose.
Look at you now. Even the richest of thoughts can only be expressed by the simplistic movements of your cells.
You still have your glasses on your neck. Is it to reminisce about old friends whom you have no right to reminisce, or to mock the dead whom you have no right to mock?
If you are Mártus the Ægir, then I hereby pass judgment upon the fallen.
If you are merely Seaborn, then we'll skip the formalities.
The organelles surrounding the Seaborn expand and parry Clementia's attack. The ceremonial sword cuts into the shadow of the coral-shaped terminal.
Mm—Gurgle.
You cut off my control of the terminal?
(Breathes scorching fire)
(Howls furiously)
Emergency transmission. This is the 10th attempt—
The Seaborn have taken over the beacon tower. We cannot stop the conversion! But someone has activated the ejection protocol for that unit—good job, whoever you are!
Bio-signatures keep soaring in number. The nests are becoming one—
Incoming Seaborn tide! Scope is the entire continental shelf! Repeat, incoming Seaborn tide! Scope is the entire continental shelf!
Emergency transmission. This is the 11th attempt—
This is Consul Clementia. Citizens of Milliarium, heed me!
Once the city unit containing the beacon tower has been fully ejected, the rest of the city shall break through the Seaborn encirclement and move towards dry land!
Reactivate the linkage mechanism in the city's framework and perform a structural reorganization. Force fields at full power! We'll stop the Seaborn tide from setting a single tentacle onto land!
Legions VIII and X, along with the Sea Patrol: send all operational warships out in turn to escort the city!
May Ægir and our forefathers be with you.
You were looking for an opportunity this whole time... consul of Ægir.
You're tough.
We... too. Once.
Is it Ægir or the Seaborn who are trying to open up a Waterway? The readings are off the charts. The endless Seaborn tide is approaching us at the very moment...
This is beyond the lighthouse's computational capacity!
Su Santidad?
I hear you.
Look in front of you.
Those deep colors between the sea and sky are a tide of Seaborn.
The wind grows quiet... There is no point in trying to estimate their numbers and scope now.
Experience is better than data. I've seen the ocean like this before.
Do you mean the... the Profound Silence?
Leave the rest to the Penal Battalion and the Inquisitors.
R-Report!
Two divisions are ready for action. Ægirian drone matrices are helping us fine-tune our defenses.
Ægir...
Sir... the ocean... Something's rising from it.
Is that... an Ægirian city?
Having sailed for thousands of miles, Milliarium rises to the surface of the ocean after casting out its heart.

Every inch of it is being reformed, supported by its framework. An invisible forcefield extends towards the bottom of the sea and the highest reaches of the sky, forming a structure like a great wall.
The structure breaks the surface of the ocean, creating a giant wave a hundred meters tall. However, on the other side of the city, the real tide has arrived—
It is a living wave. Seaborn of various forms have fused with the ocean. The children of the sea are the sea itself; they have even changed the color of the water.
The city from the depths and the roiling tide stretch out at the same time, colliding, embracing, and ripping at each others' throats.
Nature never tires, yet civilization will not stop its footsteps.
All is quiet. No wind dares tell of what it sees, here and now.
*cough*—
Phew. That woman nearly got me—
C-Cassia? I've been looking for you forever! Where'd you run off to earlier?
Lucilla?
What are you doing here, child?
The city unit framework needs servicing; it's a standard combat duty. But... look at the beacon tower... What happened inside the city?
The Seaborn have broken into Milliarium. That tower has become one of their nests.
Leave this to me. You should get out of here, quick.
It's too risky. I'll stay here with you.
No need to—Urgh.
Are you hurt?!
Yes.

It must have been the Seaborn as you came over—
Wait, no, a scratch like this couldn't be left by a Seaborn...
Did someone attack you?
*cough*!
Wait, why are you pulling out the safety valve?
Hey, don't push it! It'll cut off the ground level self-diagnostic system, and the city framework will stop working—
No, you know that way better than I do...
You've been trying to do this all along, Cassia? Are you... with the Church of the Deep?

......
When the Sea Patrol investigated Tullia, you deliberately turned them against her? Don't tell me it was you who made her disappear—
What's this you've put on my neck?
Longbeard combtooth grass. Something made by a friend in charge of raising road-side coral. Don't move. You'll die if its bristles pierce your skin. Quite painfully, if I may add.
Now—*cough*—Leave this place, child. Once it senses you've gone far enough, it'll fall off by itself.
You want to leave, don't you?
To be honest, before we became coworkers, I happened to see your assessment at the Institute of Development Planning.
You care so much about that data scientist because you've never forgotten that evacuation of Milliarium, have you?
You watched as the distant lights of the volcanic power station went out, surrounded in endless darkness, with only strange rumbles and rustling carried to you by the currents...
The ocean as it once was.
At that very moment, you were the closest to the ocean out of all of Milliarium. You, standing at the mezzanine of the dome, all alone.

The IDP assessment was that there was too great a psychological burden, which is why they agreed to transfer you from the dome system to ground level. Your so-called 'fear of heights' was just a little joke of yours.
I'm afraid that you yourself don't even realize what lies deep within your heart.
The remaining Seaborn will soon discover that this route is still open. You wouldn't want to meet them again.
Go, quickly, before it's too late.
Cassia grips the safety valve and continues to push it. She no longer looks at the joke-loving colleague by her side.
The valve stops abruptly, releasing an ear-piercing shriek. She can push it no further.
A pair of slender hands are pushing back from the other side.
Lucilla?
Hah, in any other situation, I'd definitely give you a piece of my mind, or stuff this whatever-grass into my ink bottle and tell you this sort of practical joke is beneath you.
But it's so bright... the light from where the tower sank in, from the dome above us... Every Seaborn on the whole continental shelf is rushing towards Milliarium, right?
Just what is behind those strangely colored lights?
You're not wrong, Cassia. The Seaborn have eroded away at our lives. I've experienced the truth of that myself, and I'll never forget.
I envied Tullia. Our experiences were so similar, but she was able to protect what she treasured all along. An amazing woman, really!
And look at me, I escaped the dome and got back to the ground. Before you arrived, I was even thinking of escaping from here, too.
But where would I go?
I can't let you break the framework, Cassia. I can't let the Seaborn get into any other Milliarium's unit, even if this grass thing kills me in the process!
Because... Because there's no going back for me.
*cough*...
You really are strong, child.
Laurentina's parents once told me that while the dome may be beautiful, this transparent structure is actually the true eye of the city.
In fact, I've been asking myself recently... Since we're at war right now, shouldn't the eye be on the lookout for danger?
She forces herself to look ahead, the strange colors reflecting in her pupils. The roar of the tides and the whispering of the ocean's progeny resound in her ears.
The young Ægir presses hard against the safety valve. She does not let go. She dares not let go.
Only once a firm hand presses down on her shoulder, forcing her stiff muscles to relax, does she come back to her senses.
You can let go now, Lucilla.
Cassia, she...
Temporarily paralyzed. I only managed to graze her with my shot, which is why the paralytic took so long to take effect.
Sorry for putting you at risk.
This is Consul Clementia. Citizens of Milliarium, heed me!
Once the city unit containing the beacon tower has been fully ejected, the rest of the city shall break through the Seaborn encirclement and move towards dry land!
Reactivate the linkage mechanism in the city's framework and perform a structural reorganization. Force fields at full power! We'll stop the Seaborn tide from setting a single tentacle onto land!
Legions VIII and X, along with the Sea Patrol: send all operational warships out in turn to escort the city!
It might be more appropriate if you were the one to handle the report regarding Cassia, Lucilla.
I see. You should be heading straight to the port now. The mission just declared by the consul is more urgent.
Cassia's already subdued. I'll take her to a doctor first, and then... she should be punished.

And if the city is going to undergo structural reorganization, the dome system will be the focal point of the force field to hold off the Seaborn. So they could probably use some extra hands up there...
Maybe I should submit an urgent application to return to my old post.
You're very brave, Lucilla.
Oh, so you heard all that earlier...
I did. Before you submit that application though, you ought to see a doctor yourself. Longbeard combtooth grass does in fact contain a potent neurotoxin.
Ah. Right, I will.
Ramming armor, check. Ship cannon mounts, check. Monitors, check. Camouflage coating, check. Propulsion system, check. All system diagnostics complete, ready to launch.
We'll be heading to the designated waters at full speed to block the Seaborn tide from the north.
All weapon system permissions granted. No need to hold back. Don't let them get near Milliarium.
We'll do everything we can.
You're just part of the first sortie. We'll be sending the rest of our ships into battle after you.
You're to escort Milliarium in shifts while it's reorganizing, serving as an extension of its 'Great Wall' and preventing the Seaborn from making landfall.
So remember, the moment you get the order to change shifts, you must return to resupply immediately.
Understood.
Seaborn spotted!
The remaining Seaborn in the city are attacking the port! Do not let them approach the warship docks!
(Pained convulsions)
Not content with ambushing us in their nest, they made a sneak attack on our city, too?
...How annoying.
(Rapidly flaps fins)
Wrapping around a statue? What, have the Seaborn developed a taste for art since entering the city?
That said, the Sorrowdrinker is hardly a masterpiece, so I won't feel too bad when I've reduced it to rubble.
Honestly, I think it's got more charm with an arm broken off.
Thanks for the glowing review, Skadi. Are you feeling better now?
Yeah, ever since we left the nest.
Good to hear.
It's the Hunters!
The Abyssal Hunters have safely returned to the city!
Swordfish.
Captain Gladiia, you've met...
Yes.
I saw him.
We've arrived at the right time. You must have heard Secunda's report over the emergency frequency.

Infiltrating Milliarium, breaking into the beacon tower's control room, murdering the weapons technology advisor of the Waterway Program, and finally, choosing to return to the ocean with the Seaborn.
Ulpianus has not made it easy to clear him of suspicion.
To capture him, or even execute him is a duty we Abyssal Hunters must perform in the future.
Captain Gladiia...
But for the moment, our mission is to search for the remaining Seaborn scattered around the city units, and put every last one of them to the sword.
...What must be said, I have said.
You've avoided answering the question at the heart of all this, Mártus.
The question... at the heart...?
From your description, everything that transpired beneath the ruins, the crimes you committed and the consequences incurred... They all seemed to stem from some mysterious 'inevitability.'
But the Mártus I know was an eccentric yet gifted man. He was doubtful by nature, and not one for making arbitrary decisions.
He would not have rashly discarded all reason and played at being the mystic. And he would have been even less inclined to reject his own existence, or ignore the worth of others.
The choices I have made, the outcomes I have borne.
Why do you care what the past Mártus thought, before he made his decision?
His past choices have made you who you are now. Without understanding his reasoning, we cannot understand your existence.
You would care about such trifles as well, Kal'tsit?
(The nuclei within its skull spin)
I understand now.
Of course you would care. How could you not?
The human that once ate the strange flesh experienced such struggle, too. Was it a day, or perhaps several? In the depths of the ruins, time holds no measure.
He feared. He doubted. He was compelled to think over every possibility.
Where will the next crisis come from? When will it descend upon Terra? He used a lifetime of knowledge to attempt to decipher the undecipherable, to comprehend the incomprehensible.
If there exists a lifeform that could survive the cosmos of quietus, even if only as a singular being, how would it be born?
Given infinite time, even the tiniest fin will be able to type out the ultimate truth of all things on a terminal using its tail. But time was our most precious resource.
Admittedly, the Feranmut before him had foreseen our ruinous prospects, but could He and His progeny continue bearing the torch of life through it all?
Supposing they could, before they instinctually iterate a way of overcoming that destruction in a limited time, how large a population and how efficient a fecundity would they require?
Would they have to consume a patch of sea? A whole planet? An entire star system? How many must die to support their lives?

Supposing they could not, then where did the true answer lie? On the other side of the ocean? On the other side of the sky? Or perhaps it is somewhere out in the sea of stars, untouchable by Terran hands?
Even if you spent your entire life simulating, you would not have been able to obtain a definite conclusion.
The information you possessed was simply insufficient to calculate every possibility.
Indeed, Kal'tsit. But the past Mártus had no choice but to try.
Danger and possibility both hung above his head, while below his feet he found only an unseen tightrope.
You could have informed your country from the start.

Ægir would have helped take on your burdens. The oldest records of the Academy of Science could verify the past you saw. The latest inventions of the Academy of Technology could model the future you envisioned.
To cede the choice to others. Is that a solution, or an escape?
The choices of the countless many against the choices of the few. Are they more accurate, or more ambiguous? More earnest, or more capricious?
Suppose the means for life to continue is not civilization, and the species that transcends ruin is not humanity. If so, how would Ægir react?
Enough, Mártus.
If you had thought of every possibility and foresaw the bitter price to pay, then your final decision is even more inexcusable.
No, Ægir, I do not mean to defend my choices.
What I wanted to say was that, at the time, there was an immature lifeform that interrupted my flights of fancy.
Its tentacles were minute, and its body yet uncoordinated. Beneath its translucent membranes, simple organs could be clearly seen.
Born from Ishar-mla, it had unknowingly crawled past the cabin wall, and was licking my ankle happily.
Was it unaware of how swiftly its death would come?
Did it not know that it was destined to become one of those insignificant dots among the millions of bio-signatures on the monitor?
Being the progeny of a Feranmut, could it have not seen the frightful vision its parent saw? Was it unaware that the destiny of its entire race was only to foretell and greet ruination?
It just wriggled calmly towards its foreordained destruction, acting out of instinct only.
As for us, there are countless possibilities to consider before making a decision, countless trade-offs to worry over.
We find ourselves unable to ignore the costs of our decisions, or the results, or the value and meaning of those results.
Humanity, glub—Humanity is ever a prisoner of itself, ever one step behind 'fate.'
This is the essential difference between humanity and the Seaborn. This is why they are suited to bear the duty of continued existence.

opts: That's why you ate the strange flesh?
That's why you became like this?
That's why you interfered with civilization's evolution?

lines.x: If there is any life that can survive that future of quietus, no matter what form it takes...
It is worth fighting for, no matter the cost.
'There is no justice here. Only the path ahead.'
You should understand now why you must help we many evolve.
You are not the first one the Doctor and I have seen who can barely qualify as human, but they all made their hard choices.
No one had ever made a choice as horrifying as yours.
Before you ate that flesh to become a Seaborn, you had already renounced thinking as a human does.
From my perspective, that which you abandoned in your final moments—your doubts, misgivings, concerns, and struggles—were the most precious of treasures.

opts: They're treasures because they're unending.

lines.x: The Seaborn are swallowing Terra's life whole. You have not denied this, nor can you stop it. How are we to see them as our hope?
What is your choice then, Kal'tsit? To incubate Originium?
The collapsed stargate, the extinguished sarcophagi... Of the countless failed plans, is Originium the only one still in operation?
Stones amidst the land and sky, ever silent. They are an incurable disease, harming life atop dry land.
And yet, they are a plan left by the prehistoric civilization, one expected to save all existence at the very end... In ways incomprehensible to the average person.
Glub—The stairwell to survival can only be forged in the fires of destruction.
No. Such destruction must not happen.
Much like Caerula Arbor, Originium at some point turned to the annihilation you speak of. It changed in ways none can understand, and none can reverse.
And since it has changed, it must now be stopped.
Whatever the case, using life to feed life or ruin to beget ruin would only serve to remove all meaning from survival.
...Kal'tsit. Or rather, AMa-10 from the records. You surprise me.
You and I should have arrived at the same conclusion via different paths. No. You should have been even more rational, more decisive.
I merely noticed a spark of the prehistoric civilization and took on its mantle. You, however, were born for this task. You held aloft its torch eons before I did.

You have kept watch over their distant aspirations and legacies. You have carried out your assigned mission. You witnessed changes. You bore loss... No human could have toiled for as long as you have.
But now...
Has endless 'life' rendered you feeble, Kal'tsit? Has it turned you human?

opts: Kal'tsit has always been like this.
You don't know Kal'tsit at all.

lines.x: Was it... because of this one?
Dr. Doctor, or rather... Oracle?
Many facilities, many doors, your fingerprints, face, voice... Within the cerulean cave, among the ruins... traces of you lie everywhere.
You have visited the depths of the abyss before.

opts: Have I?
A pity I don't remember...

lines.x: I recognized you. I have been speaking with Kal'tsit until now only because it is more efficient.
You must know the truth. Your visitation is sure to have meaning. Perhaps you are able to explain why the project is at a standstill.
But your title is different now; something has happened to you. Otherwise, Kal'tsit would have held no doubts about what had happened to the plan.
For several reasons, the Doctor is unable to remember many of the things back then.
(The nuclei within its skull spin)
'Memory loss'... Glub, unsurprising.
Losing one's memory is perhaps the most insignificant sacrifice you could make.
Even the most resilient of life cannot withstand the ravages of tens of thousands of years. The fact that you still exist upon Terra is in itself a miracle.
What I saw, what He saw, what They saw. Those frightful visions are an absolute truth to you.
You have lost your proud civilization and homeland. Heaven and earth have lost all color. Time and space have become vast prisons.
You have been exiled by despair. You have stopped to rest upon Terra. You have not escaped your fated ruin... All men will perish, all things rendered meaningless.
Despite this, you have not stopped fighting for hope. This planet has been forever marked by your resistance... Even if those marks are mostly wreckages.
I revere you and your cause. You, the people of a bygone age.

opts: A Seaborn acknowledges my past?
If that 'cause' involves today's Seaborn...
Maybe the project stalled because they realized the cost.

lines.x: Dr. Doctor, whether you accept it, deny it, or are undecided, it matters not to me. I am not hoping to win your understanding.
I seek not your approval.
You, and your comrades, had planted hope in the depths of the sea. A possibility. A spark.
Perhaps you have forgotten it. Perhaps you have renounced it. Perhaps you now stand against it, come what may. Now, it is I who tends to this light. And I shall not see it extinguished.
The rejection of our flame's kindler will not waver the many's resolution.
Let's stop trying to prove ourselves. We shall return to the reality itself.
We many sense the oncoming crisis. Perhaps the eye beyond the clouds has caught a glimpse of Terra's exceptionality, a sword hanging above all our heads.
Terra is but a cage, trapping countless souls unaware of the truth.
Now there is a crack in the bars. A crisis, also an opportunity.
Sight. Sound. Smell. Touch. The rushing of the ocean waves.
Within the cage that is Terra, the children of the sea are the most resilient race. Never have they stopped evolving.
You cannot deny that We Many have become the most magnificent of miracles. They will become the sparks in the wake of the Big Bang, the seeds from which the cosmos will blossom once more.
They are the closest to... success.
Tell me, Kal'tsit, Dr. Doctor, and the Ægir...
Do you now choose humanity, Originium, or the Seaborn?
It is true that a crisis fast approaches. There are still many questions I am unable to answer. Many truths I am unable to grasp.
But this does not imply that life can be reduced to a hollow concept. That would simply erase its original meaning.
You consider this a weakness, Mártus. I consider it my drive, bestowed upon me by this endless life of mine.

opts: Life's worth should be determined by the living.
Life's meaning should be written by the living.

lines.x: The Seaborn are a calamity that must be stopped.
On this, the Doctor and I will not change our positions, and neither will Rhodes Island.
Without civilization, how can you speak of survival?
Without survival, how can you speak of civilization?
This ridiculous debate has gone far enough, Mártus.
We have excised the diseased organ from Milliarium. Your 'kin' in the city are being eliminated as we speak.
You'll have plenty of time to reflect on what you've done and share the rest of your secrets with us in prison.
(Furious howl)
You, Ægir, and that ebon monster...
It is futile to contain me. You alone, your city alone, are not enough to slay me. To slay Ishar-mla.
The city has completed its reorganization. I can imagine how it will look as it bars our current, but we many will surpass it.
There is no right or wrong here. So let your brief resistance provide our soundest proof.
Next, I will rouse the slumbering Firstborn. I will lead our kin to the moons.
You will become a part of this magnificent cycle, Kal'tsit. As will you, Dr. Doctor.
Maestro.
Su Santidad, I am here to make my report.
No need. Ægir has already informed us of what transpired beneath the waves.
What of Kal'tsit and the Doctor of Rhodes Island?
They're still in the city.
We're connected, sir.
Th-This is...?
A real-time image of the sea's surface. All thanks to Ægir's drone matrix projecting directly onto the great lighthouse's display.
The Seaborn tide and the Ægirian city, both approaching the land. They're less than ten nautical miles away.
That stirring spray, those intertwined currents... Are they all... Seaborn? Even the color of the seawater has changed...
That's only the portion exposed to the surface. The tip of the iceberg.
The Seaborn took advantage of Ægir's landfall. This entire sector of sea has become their nest.
Ægir's defensive perimeter... How long can that transformed city of theirs hold?
I do not know.
Have we come to the conclusion that Ægir's Waterway Program has failed?
All the Ægir in Milliarium are still fighting as hard as they can.
For the past two months, the Penal Battalion has requisitioned all operational vessels along Iberia's coast. They've been on standby in Gran Faro, and are now ready to set sail.
I will personally lead them to reinforce Ægir's defensive perimeter. Together, we will stem the Seaborn tide.
I'm afraid you won't have much time to rest, Irene.
It seems not. I saw the Inquisitors in formation at the base of the lighthouse earlier.
In the end, Iberia has found herself walking a tightrope with no margin for error.
Survival, or utter destruction. There are no other paths before us.
Should Ægir's great wall fall, it will be up to us to educate the Seaborn on the significance of land, and the price they must pay for desecrating it.
M-Maestro! Doña Irene!
You stay here, Jordi.
This lighthouse will be our final bastion. The Penal Battalion's operations will depend on its signals. Do what you can to keep it going.
No, not that—
Look out the window.
...Light.
Is that... a lighthouse?
But aren't we at the only operational Eye of Iberia?
What's going on? Why are we suddenly receiving so many identical signals?
The lighthouses along the submerged coast, which have sat in silence for more than half a century...
They have all lit up at once!
¡Dios mío!
The silent technology of the Islanders... What are you responding to?
Ulpianus hears the rumbling of the city reorganizing above his head as he watches the lightless beacon tower slowly sinks into the depths.
The building that once supported the grand aspirations of his people is now a Seaborn brood nest.
The old and feeble will use their bodies as nutrients for the next generation. Their journeys will continue with their newborn kin.
This is how the Seaborn have always been. They spare nothing in their devotion to their kin, and do their utmost to nurture them.
The thought brings a brief moment of sickness to Ulpianus.
The weight of the great anchor in his hand stretches to his shoulder. The moment he realizes that he will never be recognized or remembered, he feels more clear-headed than ever.
He is yet unable to cut the disgusting connection between himself and the Seaborn, but he still has the strength to cut off the tide surging towards the city.
Gladiia was right. Battling in the rapidly proliferating nest might only hasten his assimilation. All that awaits him is death, or worse.
Ulpianus puts all his might into swinging his anchor.
As if able to read his thoughts, the tiny hatchlings gracefully avoid his swing before calmly flickering past the grip of his anchor. He adjusts his breathing, recalculates, and swings once more.
(Faint mechanical cry)
An immature lifeform, freshly hatched, flits across Ulpianus's vision. It flips over gracefully, revealing minute yet precise mechanical structures in its belly for just a moment.
Then there are two, then three. The school of newborns link together into a fine path of light, illuminating the chaotic waves.
Adult Seaborn of all shapes crowd around them tightly, directing the currents and protecting them as they swim into the distance.
What are you escorting, Seaborn? What is it you're protecting?
Nutrients.
Life.
Kin.
And you, Ul-pianus.
We many, together as one...
As a former consul of the Academy of Technology, one at the top of his field, Ulpianus is certain he is not mistaken. The steady flow of wondrous things streaming out of the beacon tower...
Are nanomachines once ensconced in there. A history cut off. A dream that died young.
As an Abyssal Hunter, he trusts his sense of smell as much as he does his scientific intuition. Their scent is no different from the Seaborn.
Those 'beacons,' dead in the cradle nearly two hundred years ago, now thrum with new life. Incubated by the Seaborn, they now swim together towards the tidal confluence on the surface.
Have you not yet given up on these games, Ægir, despite your own noose around your neck? Or is this also a part of your plan?
At first, there was only a mote of fluorescence.
Then, a flowing line.
And after that, an elegant lattice, spreading out across the surface of the sea.
Billions of nanomachines sway alongside the children of the sea, bobbing up and down on the ocean waves.
Their shimmer is minute yet clear, indistinct yet unwavering. The passage of light reflects about among the Seaborns' bodies.
The currents connect every nest scattered across the seas. Seaborn gather together from every corner of the ocean for their excursion onto land, gracefully extending the paths of light.
They intertwine with one another. The golden net they weave is like a galaxy strewn across the sea's surface, from Milliarium down into the deepest depths of the ocean.
The Waterway. The network of the ocean, one connecting land and sea, one transcending chaotic space and placid time.
Bound together with We Many.
There you are, Consul Clementia.
We noticed an extremely unusual bio-signature in the Sophontheatrum. I'm glad you're unharmed.
What's the status of the frontline?
Legions VIII and X, alongside the Sea Patrol, have been sending out escort ships alternatively, as per your orders.
The city's reorganization and the force fields are both complete. Milliarium is holding against the Seaborn tide.
Inform every relevant researcher in the city. I need a minute-by-minute breakdown of the situation of this region of sea.
We're still being far too passive even with our contingency plan put into motion.
Madame Consul, you may want to see what the drone matrix just sent back.
Hm?
These passages of light between the Seaborn...
'Waterways'?
Consul Clementia, we've re-established communication with the home territories! We just got a dispatch from the Institute of Scientific Development Planning.
Any news from the Hunters?
Three of them have returned to Milliarium. They're cleaning up the remaining Seaborn in the eastern city units.
Contact Gladiia. Tell her to come meet us immediately.
Yes, Madame Consul.
Let's go, Kal'tsit, Doctor.
You need to deepen your contact with Ægir.

opts: I'll go with this captain to inform the Hunters.
Kal'tsit alone will be enough for diplomacy.

lines.x: (Whisper) Doctor?

opts: (Whisper) That Seaborn left and head to the east...
(Whisper) That Seaborn might run into Skadi...

lines.x: You exchange a quick glance with Kal'tsit.
Madame Consul, the Doctor still has some affairs to settle.
...Very well.
The final battle for the Waterway Program has concluded with a Seaborn invasion of Milliarium. I require an explanation, Consul Clementia.
Gladiia.
Were any of you harmed?
I see you're here too, Kal'tsit.
We had a few unexpected encounters, but nothing we couldn't handle.
I believe that, as an envoy of Iberia, Dr. Kal'tsit also requires an explanation.
I could use one myself, if we're being honest.
There is someone who wishes to see you all.
The Little Handy moves to the center of the Pensivarium, its projection module emitting cold motes of light, slowly coalescing into the figure of an Ægir woman.
She sports a smile under her golden hair. Her eyes are focused on everyone in the room, as though she has been waiting for, or perhaps observing, them for a long time.
Consul Horatia.
Whyever do you look so fatigued, my little Gladiia?
Though the transmission efficiency of the Waterway is much higher than I anticipated, I fear our simulacra are still unable to transmit my feelings.
Otherwise I'd stroke your hair and give you a nice, big hug.
No need. You know the risks of doing so.
The changes on the sea's surface, as well as the sudden appearance of the 'Waterways'. Was this your doing?
Whether Ægir or Seaborn, we all find ourselves competing over time.
The Seaborn must keep sending nutrients to every region of sea they conquer. They wish to quickly transform the entire continental shelf into one great nest.
Every issue has two sides. This disaster has also blessed us with bio-energy beyond measure.
More than two centuries ago, the beacon launcher would have required a dozen cities' bio-energy systems to continually fabricate the nanomachines comprising the Waterways.
Now, those nanomachines are being fostered by the Seaborn, who regard them as harmless kin.
Using the data flow between the nanomachines as a medium, fleets and cities in squeezed state could traverse the oceans in an instant.
Though these machines cannot take Ægir to the stars as our forebears imagined, they are more than enough to carve a path of life for civilization in its time of need.
But that would mean that, for the foreseeable future, Ægir will have no choice but to enter into some kind of symbiosis with the Seaborn, using that... scum, as they in turn use us.
Is this another of your so-called 'games'?
It is how we will break through this siege.
The term 'Waterway,' the path ahead for Ægir, has never referred only to pure waters.
You have always had your doubts about the Program, and that is fine. But if that doubt serves only to put you in disarray, then it is useless.
As Ægir is determined to open a Waterway between sea and land, there was no question that it shall do so.
I do not doubt your success, but perhaps there is still room for debate as to your methods.
Dr. Kal'tist, I presume. Consul Horatia, of the Institute of Scientific Development Planning. It's a pleasure.
It pleases me that there are still those on dry land who have inherited the will of the prehistoric civilization. That Ægir is not alone in our duty.
I am also glad to see you have begun our discussion with a firm diplomatic stance.
Then can I take it that you speak for all terrestrial civilizations, and that we can dispense with establishing diplomacy with them one by one?
I, too, am glad to see Ægir abandon its contentment in isolation and seek out cooperation with the land.
But I regret to inform you, Madame Consul, that I do not represent it in its entirety. I merely understand it more than most.
That said, I believe what Ægir needs now is exactly a thorough understanding of the terrestrial civilizations.
You and I both know that this Waterway will not be used only for resisting the Seaborn. Ægir has its eyes on every unnatural existence in these lands.
It is the only means for us to ascertain the intrinsic nature of Terra, and to eliminate the disasters hanging above our heads at the source.
And I am sure you are aware of how such actions will be perceived by the terrestrial nations.
Compared to the Seaborn, you would be seen as the more direct threat.
We bear them no ill will. We need not stoop so low.
The cruelest thing we could do is ignore the need of help of other tottering civilizations we meet on the road to settling this crisis.
Of course, we will first help them to learn how to walk.
Bottlenecks of development, dilemmas of war, barriers of race, clashes of cultures, trends of ideology, changes in the structure of society...
Over the millennia, Ægir has grown freely beneath the waves. It has faced every issue a country might face.
We wish to share our experiences, without reservation.
And how would you do so?
Using Milliarium as our main port, we would send talent and technology ashore, to aid the Iberians in revitalizing their home.
Feranmuts perceive the oncoming crisis far more keenly than humanity. That young one will not shelter Kjerag forever.
We can aid that nation long trapped in snow in breaking through its developmental barriers in a few short years, so that it may stand equal with its neighbors.
We can aid the Sami in purging the demonic corruption permeating every inch of snow, and restart the abandoned stargate in the north.
Ursus? Kazdel? If the flames of war rage, we will calm them. If there are disputes, we will resolve them.
Bolívar? Higashi? National unity is but a few fruitful negotiations away.
Ægir will aid them in every predicament. Ægir will guide them towards the only goal they need. Under the leadership of Ægir, the land and sea will share a common future.
Madame Consul, I do not doubt Ægir's sincerity, but... I fear your proposals will not have the effect you expect.
I am sure you have the means to bring Kjerag up to par with the Core nations in a few years.
But will dialogues between equals necessarily center around peace and development?
Were Ursus's internal problems to be resolved, who is to say they would not bare their fangs once more, and attack their neighbors to satisfy their regained appetite?
Bolívar's many regimes might reach a consensus under Ægir's pressure, but how will you eradicate the profound, deliberately provoked hostility?
Dr. Kal'tsit, are you saying that Ægir's efforts to aid the terrestrial civilizations will only result in the opposite?
I am saying that we can establish a more equal and profound cooperative relationship between us.
With only your probes, you are unable to understand a civilization's needs. With only your experiences, you are unable to force a nation to skip over its path to development.
Before you act, you must learn more. Otherwise, the winds you stir on land may become some great storm you cannot turn a blind eye to.
We would be more than happy to hear your suggestions, then.
On the condition that you do not allow us to be held back by excessive caution and meaningless apprehension.
Seeing the gulf between us, between land and sea, I understand that we will need many more discussions before we can make progress.
Once the Waterway opens, I look forward to meeting you in the flesh. Whether on land, or in Ægir's home territories.
I as well, Madame Consul.
What about you, Gladiia?
Do you agree with Dr. Kal'tsit on this matter?
The IDP need not question Rhodes Island's sincere wish for cooperation, nor Dr. Kal'tsit's ability. I can vouch for both of those.
And there are indeed essential differences between Ægir and the nations of the land.
The land dwellers are shortsighted and clumsy, which makes them frail. Forcibly removing the differences between them would be no different from destroying them.
It is truly necessary to show good faith.
Hmm.
I've never seen Gladiia place so much trust in someone, Dr. Kal'tsit. A feat, to be sure.
Perhaps she can be a bridge between our peoples, and aid us in coming to a consensus.
You will see that Ægir's plans are no mere idle pratter.
Now, do allow me a moment alone with Consul Clementia.
Secunda, a fleet approaches from the land!
Iberia.
Hail them via the drone matrix.
This is Praefecta Secunda, of the Milliarium Sea Patrol.
Err... It's me.
Irene?
We're currently in a head-on battle against the Seaborn. Don't come any closer.
Your ships are too crude. I'm sorry, but they may get sucked into the eddies around Milliarium.
You're saying that Iberia should just wait quietly on land?
That I should just watch Milliarium get invaded by Seaborn, and let the Waterway transform into some kind of god-knows-what that even you Ægir never foresaw?
We've already talked about this, Secunda. I'll be there in five minutes.
Halt all attacks.
If we are going to pick up the Iberians, we'll just have to open the access channels to the rear. Why stop attacking?
Look at the screen.
The Academy of Technology just sent an urgent communique.
This...
What do we do?
Are those light-emitting nanomachines components for the Waterway?
But the Seaborn are still attacking the city. If they've fused with the nanomachines and bear identical bio-signatures, our weapons won't be able to locate and attack the Seaborn only...
Don't tell me the Legions want us to halt our covering fire and rely only on Milliarium's force fields to hold them back?
May as well slit our wrists and be done with it.
Scribbling on your device again? You know, sometimes I really admire your ability to keep a cool head.
Use the images from the drone matrix to render a 3D model.
Rendering in progress.
Perhaps the answer lies within the riddle itself.
The bio-signatures of the nanomachines and the Seaborn are identical, but we still know clearly that the nanomachines have formed a Waterway.
How?
We can see them! Each machine emits a particular light that's clearly visible!
Contact the Academy of Technology, and tell them we have a proposal for modifying our weapons' targeting systems.
But... recalibrating two Legions' worth of auxiliary optical systems would take a lot of time.
What about using a lighthouse on land?
Irene.
It seems we arrived in the nick of time. A dozen or so lighthouses on the lost coastline have resumed operation. They're... responding to your Waterway.
Combined, they should be enough to illuminate this entire patch of sea. Maybe they could serve as your auxiliary optical systems?
I'm hardly an engineer, but these lighthouses were built with Islander... er, Ægirian technology. They should be compatible with your existing systems.
That should cut the time needed in half. I'll let Consul Clementia know.
...Thank you, Irene.
The Inquisition would be overjoyed to see these newly unclouded Eyes become part of the combined defenses of land and sea. We're partners now, Secunda.
Though maybe we should be thanking Breogan instead.
Breogan...
He truly dedicated himself to these lighthouses.
A naval architect drifting into a foreign land, striving for the day when land and sea could work together. He foresaw calamity, but left no answers behind.
It's a pity that Ægir saw him as an outsider, while Iberia never truly accepted him. He died alone and miserable. But now, his plans will finally have the chance to become reality.
...We acted far slower than he did.
Indeed.
But it's not too late. Or rather, now is precisely the time.
Let's get started, Irene.
I'm still more used to you with your veil on, Horatia.
Hmm?
Did you show off the scar below your ear to let Gladiia know that, as her mother, you will tolerate her setbacks just as you accept the blemish she left on your face?
Hah, I'm not that strict a mother.
With her left hand on the astrolabe, Horatia's right hand softly strokes her cheek below her right ear, where a thin scar stretches upwards, nearly touching her eye.
There is a smile on her face.
But this is no blemish. It's beautiful. Trivial for a doctor to erase, but I refuse.
My little Gladiia was barely five at the time. She was taking her first career development assessment, and through this made her wishes rather clear.
You don't know how glad I am to see her grown.
She's certainly more suited for the life of a legionnaire than in the Institute of Social Planning or Institute of Scientific Development Planning like you expected.
No matter. Whatever field she joins, she is sure to excel. She was born for Ægir.
I remember when she was two, the Institute of Incubation said your disposition and the nature of your work made you ill-suited to raise her, and suggested you place her in their care.
They said the same when I first decided to raise her myself. Saying that again two years later would not change my mind.
But what truly pleased me was seeing young Gladiia reject it as well.
I wouldn't say you have the best bond as mother and daughter, but you do have an odd kind of rapport.
Alright, that's enough, my little manta ray. I suspect we have more pressing matters to discuss than reminiscing about my daughter.
...Can you please not call me that?
Very well, Consul Clementia.
While we spoke, every city in the home territories has been conducting an unprecedented investigation into the Church of the Deep.
As the core of R&D, Blandus was able to tamper with the Class IV weapons, but his actions should absolutely have been detected at some point before they were put to use.
Meaning there are many behind him fanning the flames.
All members of both Academies who had anything to do with the weapons' design, manufacture, and maintenance are receiving the most thorough investigations possible.
In response, the Church has brought their full influence to bear. By grasping at a single leaf, we may be able to pull out an entire rotten branch.
And with Mártus, no doubt we will be able to identify the Church's deepest roots.
...Ægir itself is undergoing a grand surgery.
Surgery to cut out a festering tumor. Isn't it?
The Waterway is open, and the fallen who corrupt our nation have been revealed. Both are of great value, as I see it.
Are you trying to console me over the superficial Waterway Program's failure? To tell me that the sacrifices Milliarium made were not without meaning?
Why do you insist on double negatives? They make you seem weak.
There is no such thing as a 'superficial' program in Ægir, Consul Clementia.
If the IDP has approved a program, it means its planning and members are entrusted with the hopes of Ægir.
We believed you are capable of using the Class IV weapons to purify our waters and open the Waterway. This has always been an official part of the program.
...Yet it failed.
The theoretical success rate of oceanic purification was never high. However, the Waterway Program was a guaranteed success.
Did I not promise you that, before you set off? Trust in me. Trust in Ægir.
I don't doubt that even if this city were to sink, consigning all ten thousand or so Ægir souls in it to the depths, the Waterway would still open without hitch.
However, of all the possibilities you've accounted for, how many call for sacrifice?
How I wish this meeting of ours never had to happen, Horatia.
Is that why you've worked yourself to the bone, my little manta ray?
I've read the reports on Milliarium. As consul for the entire city, you need not throw yourself into each and every little affair.
The stimulants you abuse will not hide the exhaustion on your face.
You really have thought of everything. As you always do, Horatia.
All these losses and sacrifices, the falls and the betrayals, even Mártus's secret... Are you not the least bit shaken?
Speaking of Mártus... You've reminded me of something.
Of what?
Mártus regards himself as the 'gardener of sin'. At times like these, he is wont to hide himself behind history.
I can infer why he'd seek cooperation from Dr. Kal'tsit, but from the way you describe it, those 'discussions' quickly devolved into a vapid declaration of war.
I find it difficult to imagine he would be satisfied with such a failure of negotiations. That is, unless he gained some other information...
We will continue to track his whereabouts.
Are you hiding something, my little manta ray?

Clementia does not answer. She instead raises her head to look at Horatia, face to face. With the simulacrum erasing the distance between them, she knows that Horatia will be able to see every detail of her expression.
But the curve of Horatia's lips do not change, her eyes are as gentle as ever, too.
Clementia hopes that her own expression is as firm as hers.
I am still the Chief Designer of Warfare on the Waterway Program before you arrive, Horatia.
Naturally.
Your calculations may have arrived at the correct outcome, but I had to handle the situation in my own way. The Academy of Science and Academy of Technology each have their own duties.
Hmm... Yes, of course.
I only hope that this obsession with self-reflection will not cause any complications. Your keen judgment must stay unclouded.
I won't let that happen.
*sigh* Here, come a little closer. We still have some time.
Kal'tsit, Rhodes Island, Iberia, the Inquisition... You'll be busier than ever soon, so best not keep frowning like that...
(Rapidly convulses its gelatinous body)
(Quizzically sways fine tentacles)
Trying to run?
Smart. Your kin are behind this wall. A lot of them.
It's the end of the road for you, though. What a pity.
Ishar-mla?
Ishar-mla.
A sudden voice, tinged with surprise.
Skadi turns, looking towards its source. A conical creature exuding light leaves the shadows of the buildings, shielding the two cornered Seaborn with its body.
Leave, kin.
Through the eddy ahead, return to the sea.
You're so honestly gentle with them. Disgusting.
I'm guessing you're the leader?
Our meeting was preordained, Ishar-mla.
Huh? You know me?
Skadi grips her sword tightly.
Ishar-mla.
From Sal Viento to Gran Faro, from the Stultifera to here. You scum have been watching me this whole time.
What, do you see your 'god' in me too? Are you here for 'family,' or revenge?
(The nuclei within its skull rotate slightly)
No.
Ishar-mla... Ska-di, you are not nutrients to complete the cycle. You are its end.
Our kin will not prey on you.
On the contrary, they respect you. Protect you.
They await your transformation. They await your evolution.
Skadi looks at the being's 'eye.' For whatever reason, this monster she has never seen before seems familiar. Very familiar.
The bizarre organs surrounding the Seaborn disperse and surround her in turn. A shimmering 'umbilical cord' connects her body with the Seaborn's.
Skadi suddenly remembers when she faced the first Seaborn to talk, in that cave below the church. She also experienced a moment of distraction back then.

She had just been through a fierce battle, her blood boiling with frenzy. But now, she feels herself uncontrollably responding to the Seaborn's touch... She curses herself for this moment of distraction.
...Why?
A natural phenomenon. One I am also helpless to resist. This is the answer, Ska-di.
I ate of His flesh, and He lives within you.
You are He. I am He.
Our hearts connected. Our blood interlinked.
You need not waver. You need not doubt. The kin will not demand you walk with us.
Ishar-mla chose you. Chose you to continue His life. Perhaps this is the only way He will witness what He must witness. Travel the road He must travel.
What did He see in you?
Perhaps, perhaps...
Could it be, Ska-di, that in that cold, dark future, in the cosmos where all things are doomed to fade away, you are the one hope, the spark, the possibility...
Are you the one who bears the final wishes of untold lives?

opts: Skadi.
Abyssal Hunter Skadi.
Rhodes Island Operator Skadi.

lines.x: Doctor?
Skadi swings her sword, her grip never once relaxed. It slices through the 'umbilical cord.' She stares coldly at the creature before her.

opts: Everyone's looking for you.
Figured you'd be here.

lines.x: Dr. Doctor, Ska-di. You... know each other?
Is that so strange?

opts: She's one of my cute little operators.
We met on land.

lines.x: (The nuclei within its chest pulse)
This is the first time something stirs the peace within the Seaborn's chest.
The oddly-colored nuclei, along with the bizarre organs floating by their side, begin to wave as rhythmically as the tides.
It does not approach, but both you and Skadi can sense a burst of emotion: joy.
No matter if you personally took part, Dr. Doctor, no matter if what took place in the cerulean cave had aught to do with you...
After countless, countless years, you now stand alongside Ishar-mla.
The connection between you and the kin cannot be erased. It is you who wrote this instinct into our memories. We depend on one another.
The wheel of fate still turns. The surviving successors will one day meet, and achieve the most improbable of possibilities—

lines.st: The hardship we suffer is ever present.
The life we thirst for is ever present.
I await you at the endpoint of evolution, Dr. Doctor, Ska-di. I await you both.

opts: Don't give chase, Skadi.
Handle things in the city first.

lines.x: I couldn't understand that thing at all. What was it talking about?

opts: Some kind of declaration of war.

lines.x: Didn't sound like that to me.
You woke me up just now, Doctor.
When I was face-to-face with that Seaborn, I remembered something I felt back in Sal Viento.

opts: Nothing good, I presume?
I assume you weren't a fan?

lines.x: I hated it.
What good could come from a Seaborn rambling to a human?

opts: Don't worry. I know what happened.
You didn't want to listen, did you?

lines.x: Good. I couldn't explain even if I wanted to.
You don't seem surprised, though. No wonder Kal'tsit asked you to come underwater.
Of course not!
I have no interest in chatting with a Seaborn.
That thing sounded like it'd been looking for you?

opts: It's a long story.

lines.x: ...So we were both being watched.

opts: There won't be a next time.
Next time, I'll be by your side.
It'll be you waking me up next time.

lines.x: Mm.
Don't worry, Doctor. I'm Skadi. Abyssal Hunter Skadi. Rhodes Island Operator Skadi. Always have been.
Doesn't matter if that thing comes looking for me or you.
I'll cut its vocal cords before it can speak.

opts: I trust you, Skadi.
Remember who you are, and it'll work out.

lines.x: ...The tide's coming in, outside these walls.
Sounds like it won't stop for a long while.
Do you really mean to return?
I've finally found out that man's whereabouts.
No matter who he is or what vulpohole he's decided to hide himself in, he hurt our daughter. He needs to pay.
Then let me come with you. I don't care if it's famiglie standing in our way, cruel Catastrophes, endless tides, or fire raining from the skies, we'll face them together.
Would the shrine be alright without you?
Relax, it's just killing a few people. It long past time for those bastards to head to hell.
Then it seems we must part once more...
Heavens drink their fill, sky besot by sakura, drunken clouds stumble.
Was that a haiku? It's got a good rhythm, perfectly suited for reciting while swinging a sword.
(Blows nose)
The hell, man, why're you crying?
An assassin gets revenge for her daughter by crossing two big famiglie, so she's forced to flee to a distant country.
And many years later, she has to leave behind her gentle husband and re-enter the storm, all for her daughter's sake again.
When I think about the people in real life who live like that, I can't help it... The way I see it, the movie should be from her point of view!
I hope I don't gotta remind you that this film's supposed to be our famiglia's business card in the new city. And we're supposed to be reviewing it together.
We gotta control how this city looks at our boss and our business.
I thought you said it was great last time?
Great? Then why'd that killer lady show up outta nowhere?
'Cruel Catastrophes'? 'Endless tides'? 'Fire raining from the skies'? Who wrote this crap?
Sure, the Columbians poked a hole in the sky, but I ain't never heard of no fire raining down. When's this supposed to take place, anyway?
When're you gonna learn to have a bit of perspective?
A cold-blooded killer putting down her blade for a peaceful tomorrow in Nuova Volsinii, all thanks to our famiglia's influence. You can't get more with the times than that!
Eh, if you put it that way, the film's even more crap. But you're right, as long as it's to the Signora's liking—
Hey, which bastard touched the switch? We under attack?!
No, look at the screen!
Mr. Tin Man?
We have a press conference starting soon, so please keep it quick.
While you all were busy flinging all those things into the sky, I made a few rounds in Kazdel.
You know how I feel about this.
I do, young Banshee, I do.
My verdict is that they feel about you much the same as you feel about them.
Considering the many vile connections they have across Terra, if they do decide to get behind your project, there will be plenty of headaches for all involved.
Thanks for the tip. Now, the press conference is about to begin—
Don't be so hasty. Let me finish.
Nasti Lunorey, Director of Engineering at Rhine Lab.
You know full well that the dream you were promised can be shared with more than just the Sarkaz.
The Maylander Foundation will pledge their full support towards you and your Elf friend's brazen plan.
We will decide on a date to discuss the details later. That includes future launch plans, where we hope to re-establish a more complete—
Galleria Stellaria.
Yes, that's the one.
Nasti? You on the phone?
Once the conference is over, we'll need a meeting room, a coffee machine, and to clear an entire weekend off our schedule.
That bad, huh?
If only it were as simple as good or bad.
But first, the conference. Is Silence here?
I was hoping she'd stop by, but fat chance she's got the time.
I put one of those burdenbeast blind boxes from Kjerag on her desk, and sure, she'd opened it after a week, but the little beasty was already buried under an avalanche of files in need of review!
The guy who's supposed to be making a speech for us isn't here yet either.
Well, he's still got two minutes—
(Clears throat)
Ladies and gentlemen, the press conference is about to begin. Please quiet down.
I'll be happy to answer any and all relevant questions during the Q&A segment.
Sounds like he's in a good mood.
Let us first—
Playing the same trick twice? Doesn't it get old?
No, Ferdinand's confused too! This wasn't part of his plan! This is—
...The challenges we face are far greater than ourselves or Catastrophes. We must form a community...
...under the leadership of Ægir to overcome them together.
Repeat.
...an Ægir broadcast to all surface-dwelling peoples. We ask all of humanity to set aside your conflicts and prejudices, and join the Ægir in building a bulwark together.
The matrix has broken through the blockade and swept past the sea. Essential information and calculation results are about to make landfall.
The challenges we face are far greater than ourselves or Catastrophes. We must form a community...
...under the leadership of Ægir to overcome them together.
...'Under the leadership of Ægir.'

You're still looking towards the land, Dr. Kal'tsit.
Hmm... Like a mother, anxious about the child she had to leave behind.
A child, is it?

In one brief communication, you have delivered knowledge that countless others have given their lives for to no avail. Geographical data on the Foehn Hotlands and Infy Icefield, and images from even further north of the continent.
One way of 'showing goodwill,' I suppose?
To share knowledge is natural, while showing goodwill takes intention. I fail to see how the two are related.
It was essential to thoroughly evaluate the extent of shock this would be to our terrestrial friends. Were these not urgent times, we really should have been more cautious.
My concern is that—
There are immediate dangers more worthy of our attention.
How much longer do you believe Milliarium can hold, Consul Clementia?
With our fleets taking shifts, we can keep up continuous, twenty-four-hour covering fire for a week.

There have been no major changes in the Seaborn's movements. As the energy supplying the force fields dries up, the stopping power of our weapons will also gradually dwindle... but this is not outside expectations.
The city can hold for another half a month. No more.
How long will it take reinforcements to arrive from the home territories?
A week.
Our countless nanomachines have established steady information flows. Compressed fleets and cities can speed through them, traversing the oceans in an instant.
However, assembling our forces will still take time.
A week... How quick.
You need not hide your worries, Dr. Kal'tsit.
There are essential differences between the ways we and the landwalkers think. Simple sincerity is insufficient for reaching effective bilateral cooperation.
On the one hand, we hope that reinforcements will arrive as soon as possible. On the other, you worry that the subsequent contact and communications will be outside your control.
You even worry that Skadi and the Doctor have deviated from your plans.
You already know of Skadi's secret, then.
My Little Handy happened to record a chance meeting between Skadi, the Doctor, and Mártus on a street corner.
Compared to discovering the secrets of their identities, controlling who knows what is much more difficult.
An Abyssal Hunter with a Firstborn within her, and a Predecessor scientist who witnessed the rise and fall of the prehistoric civilization in person. They are both of great import to Ægir.
Based on simple evolutionary logic, the Firstborn would not have chosen to entrust the future of its kin to its killer.
We still have no idea how the Seaborn will become the final answer to resisting ruination.
Even with missing memories, the Doctor still bears that mission. How will this so-called 'death of all things' come about, and what can Terra do about it? We must find out.
Skadi and the Doctor are, without a doubt, the key to Ægir's solving of the riddles.
Skadi will face her blood. The Doctor's duty will be fulfilled. But it should be their choice, not somebody else's scheme.
I fear that once Ægir learns of their identities, it will devise ways to exploit them.
Horatia asked me earlier if I was hiding something from her.
And how did you answer, Madame Consul?
No need to worry. She does not know, yet.
You truly treasure Skadi and the Doctor, don't you? Even when confronted with a disaster threatening to wipe out civilization, you still wish to respect their choices.
But this is no time for us to hesitate. I can't hide this from Horatia forever.
I presume you have a plan then, considering you're hiding this from her at all.

I've reached an agreement with Gladiia. We will take over all Abyssal Hunter-related research in the name of the Legions, and ensure that the Hunters will not be put in circumstances depriving them of a choice.
As for the Doctor and myself... We hope to go deeper into the Ægir home territories, and solve these mysteries by ourselves.
I fear that we will need an Ægir guide.
Well then... it would be my pleasure.
Cassia will receive the punishment she deserves. Milliarium's incubation chamber has been moved to safety, so as long as the city doesn't fall, the child she left behind will be born safely.
They will be raised in the Institute of Incubation.
I know what you're thinking. The fate of a Church of the Deep cultist isn't worth calling you over for.
Do you still think that everything will end in ruin, Mr. Avitus?
I still believe in history. Once written, it can never be changed.
Even a tragic victory is destined to be another page in its annals.
I'm glad to hear you say that.
Let's get down to business, Jordi. I met with Consul Clementia.
I confessed everything. What I was thinking, what I did, what I saw but never spoke of.

I accepted the tribunal's interrogation, but they did not believe I deserved to be labeled fallen. I had stood at the precipice and looked around. Perhaps even stuck a toe over the edge, but no more than that.
Their final verdict was that I could keep my post and continue to stay in Milliarium.
It sounds like you have other plans, though?
They may see it as repentance, but I cannot forget the outcome of my silence and inaction.
I'm not arrogant enough to believe I could have affected an entire military campaign on my own, but... what if I had reported Cassia's unusual behavior?
This could have played out very differently.
And so, I plan to leave Milliarium.
To where? The Ægirian home territories?
Somewhere even further than that.
Even further?
Now that the Waterway has opened, Ægir is about to restart the work interrupted by the Seaborn.
Expeditionary fleets will once more explore the far side of Terra, but this time, we have changed the course of our voyage.
We might set off from that mining nation of Rim Billiton, and try to touch the edge of the sea.
Better I die on some expedition into the unknown than wallow here in sorrow, self-reproach, and despair until it saps me of all my strength.
There, whether we go forwards or downwards, no one knows what awaits us. We will be like Breogan in times of yore, pushing back against the darkness of ignorance.
Y-You mean...?
You caught on, Jordi Fontanarossa. Yes, I'm inviting you to join us on this distant voyage.
I'm not the only one who desires this.
You said earlier that I never answered your questions properly. You were right, and I should apologize. But there was something I truly wished to tell you back then.
We have no need to prove our place. Even if the vestiges of the past have worn you down to nothing, you can still tie your future to Ægir.
I don't know what to say, this is all a bit much...
Don't worry. Ægir respects the choices of all its citizens.
Farewell on your carefully-considered departure. We await your eventual return, no matter how long it may take.
...Maestro.
Today's maintenance work on the lighthouse should be finished. Is there some sort of issue with the others?
No, I'm just here to watch the sea, like you.
I heard from the other Inquisitors that you kept making time to come to the coast, even while we were in Milliarium?
Iberia threw a stone into the sea. We were worried it would foment an unstoppable wave, but what was even more worrying was that it might sink in silence, with nary a whisper.
Fortunately, the stone stirred up the ripples we hoped for, and Iberia was able to see the circumstances beneath the waves... and in so doing make up its mind.
You performed your duties as envoy admirably. If there was ever a time in your life to stand tall with your head held high, now is that time.
Y-Yes, sir!
Maestro, I've been thinking about returning to Gran Faro. Going back to the chapel, and where I used to live with Uncle Thiago.
...You should have seen Thiago one last time.
We live in an age of regrets. If you see the Inquisition as the source of those regrets, I cannot refute you.
If you have things to pack, Irene can help you.
What? No, that's not what I meant. I'm really just thinking of... going back to have a look.
In other words, you haven't yet made up your mind.
Yeah...
You told me earlier that the Inquisition letting me to return to Ægir was a test. Am I not...
On the contrary, Jordi. Do not mistake bewilderment for momentary hesitation.
You have lifted your lantern and clearly seen the fork in the road before you. You are merely weighing your choices before choosing which path to follow.
The sea fog is thick, and both routes seem slick with mud. But you are not the one to be criticized for this.
Jordi Fontanarossa.

If you choose to follow that Ægirian scholar into unknown waters, the Inquisition will not stop you. The strength of the waves crashing against the shore will not change because a single clerk leaves or stays.
If you choose to stay ashore in Iberia, you will carry responsibilities that are more exhausting than you are used to, but also much more important.
Those are the expectations Iberia has for you.
The choice is yours.
I... I understand, Maestro.
By the by, Irene mentioned that you've been suffering from severe insomnia these past few days. How was last night?
It rained rather heavily around midnight, without a star in the sky. By the crack of dawn, there had been about two or three urgent situations I had to handle.
But overall, a rather peaceful night, all things considered.
Do the Seaborn dream?
Dreams are comprised of thoughts or emotions flaring up as they dissipate, but now I am nothing more than instinct.
Why... this scene... again...
Following the unfathomable passages in the ruins, at the very end, a great beast gently sticks a tentacle to the bulkhead. What is it waiting for? Perhaps...
I walk forward, passing through the shadow of the cerulean tree, until I am before Him. I extend a hand.
(Unknown language) Warning. Deep sleep procedures will commence in three minutes.
(Unknown language) Real-time translation programs will soon shut down. Please provide all necessary instructions as soon as possible.
(Unknown language) Ishar'mla, our shared name... our shared decision.
(Unknown language) We trust one another. We feel as one, do we not?
(Unknown language) I will watch over you, until the end of my days.
No, this person isn't me.
This isn't my dream. It's... Ishar-mla's memories?
Guh.
You have followed me through many nests, Abyssal Hunter.
The reinforcements from the home territories are about to arrive. The confrontation between your kin and Ægir's Legions will soon intensify. You would choose this moment to depart?
Your frontline has already advanced onto land, Seaborn. Why choose to turn back and return to the abyss?
How is this conflict connected to the reawakening of the Firstborn?
You are an astute man, Hunter.
I merely discovered your existence before the others.
You left evidence in Ishar'mla's body.
You are the Church of the Deep's... founder.
You sought refuge in the shadows to seek traces of me?
Only a shadow may merge with a shadow.
A most interesting Hunter.
What do you wish to know?
Everything.
I know you, Hunter. You are Ska-di's comrade.
Has Ægir refused your everything?
This nation has refused too much. So much so that it's dug its own grave.
Your schemes are far too obvious.
I never intended to hide them.
I fell in order to obtain answers.
(The nuclei within its skull converge)
I can tell you all you've longed to know, Hunter.
Regarding my next actions, regarding the sealed passages in the ruins embedded in Terra's mantle, regarding how the other Firstborns will awaken...
Regarding Ægir, regarding the next doom to greet Terra...
We kin are selfless. We kin are devoted. We kin wish to share everything.
The only requirement is that you become one with we many.
Ulpianus meets the Seaborn's 'gaze.'
It has no face, but he can tell that it is observing him. Through its transparent membranes, past the faintly leaping nuclei, Ulpianus sees his own eyes.
You said you 'fell,' earlier?
No, Hunter. The conjoining of flesh is not enough.
Turn endurance to acceptance. Turn humiliation to elation. You must trust the kin... Trust your own instincts.
You must take up a duty far beyond imagination. The duty of we many.
Perhaps you will learn the full truth in our embrace. But when the time comes, you will be unable to, unwilling to, bring it back to Ægir.
...Just as I could not.
The Seaborn passes Ulpianus, swimming ever deeper into the nest. Its back quickly disappears from view, leaving only the nest before him in full bloom.
The contaminated coral shimmers in its multitudes. Countless fantastical lifeforms flit between them, using its glimmer to dispel the darkness of the depths. They grow. They form tides.
But Ulpianus ignores them. His gaze passes over a golden sea, over every scar, every inch of flesh, every cell, every chaotic waking dream...
It falls somewhere he cannot see. The deepest recess of his own self.
Ulpianus follows along.
